questions.q: In the relativist theory of right conduct, an action A by a member of culture C is OBLIGATORY iff:
options: A produces good consequences for C
A is required by the basic moral norms of C
A is permitted by some other culture
A maximises overall utility
A is prohibited by C's norms
questions.explain: MR fixes obligatoriness by what C's basic moral norms REQUIRE. Other options describe alternative theories.

questions.q: Which best states Moral Relativism (MR) as defined in the lecture?
options: What is right or wrong for the members of a culture is ultimately determined by the basic moral norms of their culture
No moral claims are ever correct
Every culture accepts the same basic moral norms
Moral norms apply only to isolated individuals, never to cultures
Moral claims report mere personal preference
questions.explain: MR's core thesis: the basic moral norms of a culture are the ultimate standard for that culture.

questions.q: Moral Objectivism (MO) holds that:
options: All cultures in fact accept the same norms
At least some moral norms are correct independently of what any culture accepts and apply to all cultures
Only one culture has the correct moral code
Moral norms reduce to natural regularities
Moral norms are determined by majority vote within a culture
questions.explain: MO's hallmark is culture-INDEPENDENT correctness with universal scope.

questions.q: The Moral Diversity Thesis (MD) claims:
options: Every culture has its own dialect
Moral codes are uniform across cultures
The moral codes of some cultures include basic moral norms that conflict with those of other cultures, and such conflicts are widespread
Different cultures use different languages to express the same norms
Cultures sometimes disagree about non-moral facts
questions.explain: MD has TWO components — conflict in BASIC norms, and that such conflict is WIDESPREAD.

questions.q: Context Sensitivity (CS) says:
options: What is right depends on majority opinion
What is right depends on the basic norms of a culture
All actions are right in some context
What is right or wrong for someone often depends on circumstances, including facts about their culture
Right and wrong are illusions
questions.explain: CS is about circumstances; importantly it is COMPATIBLE with MO, unlike MR.

questions.q: What is the Principle of Tolerance as stated in the lecture?
options: Cultures should compete openly
It is morally wrong for any culture to interfere with the moral practices of another culture
Tolerance is a personal virtue, not a moral rule
No culture should ever change its norms
All cultures are equally praiseworthy
questions.explain: The principle of tolerance is presented as a universal prohibition on cross-cultural interference.

questions.q: On MR, when a member of a culture publicly opposes its practice in order to reform its code, that reformer is:
options: Morally heroic
Morally optional
In the wrong
Acting outside morality altogether
Necessarily correct
questions.explain: By MR, the reformer's act conflicts with C's current basic norms, so it is wrong by MR's own standard.

questions.q: In the relativist theory of right conduct, an action A is OPTIONAL iff:
options: A is forbidden by some culture
A is neither required nor prohibited by the basic moral norms of C
A maximises utility
A is required by every culture
A is required by some culture
questions.explain: Optional = neither obligatory nor wrong on C's code.

questions.q: Which of the following statements about MR and MD is correct?
options: MR entails MD
MD entails MR
MR can be true even if all cultures happen to share the same basic norms
MR is identical to MD
MD is identical to CS
questions.explain: Lecture analogy: everyone might drive on the right and it still be conventional. Universality of practice does not refute relativism.

questions.q: Which thinker's name is most closely associated with the formulation of the principle of utility?
options: Plato
Aquinas
Hobbes
Confucius
Bentham
questions.explain: Decoy/orientation question — Bentham is the utilitarian touchstone introduced in Lecture 5, included here as a recall distractor to keep MR from being confused with utilitarianism.

questions.q: The expert-swimmer-vs-non-swimmer beach example is used in the lecture to illustrate that:
options: MR is true
Cultures all share basic norms
CS is not equivalent to MR — different actions can be required by a single objective principle when non-moral facts differ
Swimming is a moral obligation
Drowning children are not moral patients
questions.explain: The same objective norm ('help those in need when you can without serious risk') prescribes different actions to different agents.

questions.q: In the lecture, a NON-FUNDAMENTAL moral disagreement is one in which:
options: The parties accept different basic moral norms
The parties accept the SAME basic moral norm but differ in their non-moral beliefs
The parties have no real disagreement at all
Both parties are lying
Both parties believe contradictories simultaneously
questions.explain: Same norm + different non-moral beliefs (e.g., effects of capital punishment) — in principle resolvable by empirical evidence.

questions.q: A FUNDAMENTAL moral disagreement is distinguished from a non-fundamental one by:
options: Its emotional intensity
Whether the parties speak the same language
Its resolvability by rational means — fundamental disagreements cannot be settled even with full agreement on non-moral facts
Whether it concerns punishment
Whether it concerns adults
questions.explain: The criterion is resolvability: fundamental disagreements remain even after non-moral facts are agreed upon.

questions.q: In the Anthropologist's Argument version 1, the most direct response to premise P2 ('if MD is true, no universal moral norms') is:
options: Reject MR for being inconsistent
Note that mere difference of BELIEF over some issue does not imply absence of a single correct belief
Argue that MD is false
Show that CS holds
Show that the principle of tolerance is universal
questions.explain: Analogy with disagreement over the earth's shape: belief-difference doesn't refute objectivity.

questions.q: The Anthropologist's Argument version 2 differs from version 1 chiefly by:
options: Adding that intercultural conflicts cannot be resolved by rational means
Replacing MD with CS
Adding a tolerance premise
Dropping the conclusion that MR is true
Adding that all cultures use the same language
questions.explain: Version 2 strengthens the inference by claiming the conflicts are rationally irresolvable — best-explained by absence of objective norms.

questions.q: The 'chicken pull' case is offered to:
options: Defend MR
Show that animals have rights
Illustrate an objectivist response: insist the agent is mistaken and urge sympathy
Illustrate the principle of tolerance
Refute MD
questions.explain: Disagreement does not show no fact of the matter; the right response can be to call the practice wrong.

questions.q: The 'Susan' example (Catholic + Democrat) is used to argue that MR has a problem of:
options: Determinacy
Tolerance
Reformer status
Consistency: the action would be both right and wrong
Hedonism
questions.explain: A member of multiple cultures with conflicting basic norms gets contradictory verdicts on the same action.

questions.q: The Usability & Determinacy problem for MR is that:
options: MR is too vague about what counts as a culture (by language? ethnicity? religion? politics?), so it cannot specify what makes an action right
MR allows too much tolerance
MR has too few applications
MR is empirically false
MR cannot accommodate context sensitivity
questions.explain: Without a determinate account of 'culture', MR cannot fix any verdict.

questions.q: The Intercultural Evaluation Problem for MR is:
options: That every culture is internally consistent
That MR makes it incoherent to question the correctness of a culture's norms from outside, even though such questioning clearly makes sense
That MR conflicts with the principle of tolerance
That MR cannot apply to nomads
That MR requires cultural homogeneity
questions.explain: On MR a culture's norms ARE the ultimate standard, so external criticism would be confused — but we evidently engage in such criticism intelligibly.

questions.q: The Intracultural Evaluation Problem for MR concerns:
options: Refugees moving between cultures
Comparative claims like 'our morality has improved' or 'declined', which MR struggles to make sense of because the standard itself changes with the code
The translation of moral terms
Disagreements among cultural elites only
Mass media's role in morality
questions.explain: Comparative judgments over time need a standpoint stable enough to compare past and present codes — MR provides none.

questions.q: The capital-punishment example with shared utilitarian framework but disagreement about consequences shows that:
options: Capital punishment is morally permissible
Capital punishment is morally wrong
A surface moral disagreement can be NON-fundamental — same basic norm, different non-moral beliefs about consequences
MD is true
MR is false
questions.explain: This is the textbook case for re-describing 'moral' disagreement as empirical disagreement.

questions.q: Suppose two parties share NO non-moral disagreements yet still disagree about whether an action is right. By the lecture's classification this is:
options: Trivial
A misunderstanding of language
A non-fundamental disagreement
A fundamental moral disagreement
Evidence for MR by itself
questions.explain: Fundamental disagreements survive agreement on non-moral facts. (Note: fundamental disagreement does not by itself entail MR.)

questions.q: Which of the following is the BEST objection to inferring MR from widespread fundamental disagreement?
options: MD is empirically certain
MD is not even meaningfully formulated
Widespread fundamental disagreement is not firmly established and many surface disagreements are non-fundamental
Tolerance entails objectivism
Cultures do not actually exist
questions.explain: Most intercultural disputes can be plausibly re-described as non-fundamental, so the empirical premise of MD is shaky.

questions.q: Restricted Moral Relativism (Restricted MR) holds that:
options: MR is rejected
Right and wrong depend on a culture's norms only insofar as those norms satisfy universal constraints from the functions of morality and facts about human nature
Right and wrong depend purely on individual choice
All cultures must accept the same basic norms
Only restricted MR can accept MD
questions.explain: Restricted MR is pluralistic but not 'anything goes': culture-independent constraints filter admissible codes.

questions.q: In Restricted MR, the interpersonal function of morality is said to yield which norm in particular?
options: A norm of self-interest
A norm of equality of outcome
A norm of reciprocity (returning good for good) that encourages other-regarding behaviour
A norm of absolute tolerance
A norm of universal love
questions.explain: Reciprocity is highlighted as the central interpersonal norm flowing from morality's social function.

questions.q: Why is UNRESTRICTED MR INCOMPATIBLE with the universal Principle of Tolerance?
options: Because tolerance is itself a culture-specific value, yet the principle presents itself as universal — and unrestricted MR cannot endorse any universal norm
Because tolerance is a non-moral value
Because tolerance contradicts itself
Because tolerance requires force
Because tolerance is non-rational
questions.explain: A universal 'don't interfere' norm is precisely the kind of culture-independent norm unrestricted MR denies.

questions.q: Which statement about the relation between MR, MO, and CS is correct?
options: MR entails CS and MO
CS entails MR
CS is incompatible with MO
CS is compatible with MO; CS is NOT equivalent to MR
MO entails MR
questions.explain: A single objective principle can prescribe different actions to differently situated agents — CS rides happily on MO.

questions.q: The 'internal support' problem for MR is best illustrated by:
options: Nazi Germany's antisemitism conflicting with one's considered moral beliefs — MR cannot adequately explain why we treat the cultural code as wrong
A culture failing to teach its norms to children
A society lacking written laws
Disputes about etiquette
Cross-cultural translation difficulties
questions.explain: MR fails to support our considered judgement that the cultural code itself was wrong; this failure of internal support is also a failure of explanation.

questions.q: Restricted MR is described as pluralistic in the sense that:
options: Anything goes
Only one code is true and all others are merely tolerated
Multiple codes that yield different verdicts can be equally correct, PROVIDED they satisfy the culture-independent constraints
All codes are equally true without restriction
Codes are true only relative to political parties
questions.explain: Pluralism with filters — not relativism without limits.

questions.q: Which of the following best refutes the inference 'unresolved disagreement implies no objective norms'?
options: The mere existence of a disagreement
Pointing to historical disputes that LATER turned out to have an objectively correct answer (e.g., the earth's shape), showing that unresolved-at-time does not imply no-fact-of-the-matter
The fact that some disagreements are fundamental
The principle of tolerance
The chicken-pull case
questions.explain: From 'we haven't resolved it' you cannot validly infer 'there is no answer'. Past disputes show otherwise.

questions.q: In Natural Law Theory, moral laws are called 'natural' because:
options: They describe regularities in physical nature
They are grounded in human nature
They are not written down
They are obeyed by animals
They are decided by majority vote
questions.explain: NLT uses the normative sense of 'law', anchored in features of human nature, not the descriptive sense.

questions.q: Which of the following is NOT one of the three components of NLT?
options: A perfectionist theory of value
The Principle of Double Effect (PDE)
Moral absolutism
Hedonistic calculus
questions.explain: Hedonistic calculus belongs to Bentham's utilitarianism, not NLT.

questions.q: On Aquinas's view, the value of a thing consists in:
options: Its rarity
Its hedonic intensity
The perfection of its state in performing its function (its purpose)
Its market price
Its symmetry
questions.explain: Aquinas links essence to purpose; goodness is the perfection of an item's function.

questions.q: Which list correctly names the four basic human goods in NLT?
options: Pleasure, Power, Wealth, Honor
Life, Procreation, Knowledge, Sociability
Justice, Mercy, Wisdom, Courage
Faith, Hope, Love, Charity
Liberty, Equality, Fraternity, Solidarity
questions.explain: The four basic goods anchor the fundamental principle of right conduct in NLT.

questions.q: The Principle of Double Effect (PDE) applies to:
options: Actions with only good effects
Actions with only bad effects
Actions that have at least one good effect and one evil effect
Actions performed in groups
Actions in war only
questions.explain: PDE is the framework for evaluating an action with mixed (good and evil) effects.

questions.q: Moral Absolutism (MA), as defined in the lecture, holds that:
options: Some general types of action are always morally wrong, even when they would bring about good results
All moral judgements depend on culture
Right action maximises utility
Killing is sometimes obligatory
Lying is always required
questions.explain: MA disallows trade-offs for paradigm cases such as intentionally killing the innocent.

questions.q: According to NLT's theory of right conduct, an action A is OBLIGATORY iff:
options: Performing A would result in a direct violation of a basic good
Failing to perform A would result in a direct violation of one or more basic goods
Performing A maximises utility
A is permitted by every culture
A is preferred by the agent
questions.explain: Failure to act is what would yield direct violation, so the act is required.

questions.q: The 'Fundamental Principle of Right Conduct' in NLT says:
options: Maximise pleasure
Follow your culture's norms
Preserve and promote the basic goods; avoid their hindrance and destruction
Tolerate other cultures
Cultivate virtue
questions.explain: This is NLT's foundational normative principle, stated directly in the lecture.

questions.q: In NLT, a DIRECT violation of a basic value is one that:
options: Seriously hinders or destroys a basic value AND cannot be justified by PDE
Is performed in public
Is performed without consent
Maximises overall utility
Is committed unintentionally
questions.explain: Direct violation is the morally impermissible category; indirect violations are PDE-justifiable.

questions.q: Which philosopher's name is most closely associated with NLT in the lecture?
options: Hume
Kant
Aquinas
Mill
Hobbes
questions.explain: Aquinas is the central figure for the Thomistic Natural Law Theory presented.

questions.q: Which of the following is NOT one of the three PDE conditions?
options: Intrinsic permissibility of the action apart from its effects
Non-intentionality of the bad effect (not as end or means)
Proportionality of the good to the evil effect
Maximisation of expected utility
All three of the listed PDE conditions are correct
questions.explain: PDE rejects pure consequentialism. Maximising expected utility is not one of its three conditions.

questions.q: In NLT, the hysterectomy case (removing a cancerous uterus from a pregnant patient) is morally permissible because:
options: The operation maximises utility
The action itself is permissible, the unborn's death is neither end nor means, and proportionality is met
Saving one life always outweighs another
The patient consents
The unborn has no moral status
questions.explain: All three PDE conditions are satisfied — operation is intrinsically permissible, bad effect unintended, proportionate reason.

questions.q: The craniotomy case differs from hysterectomy because:
options: The patient's consent is absent
The hospital lacks resources
The unborn's death is a MEANS of saving the mother, so the bad effect is intended — PDE condition (Non-intentionality) fails
Proportionality is not met
Intrinsic permissibility is not met
questions.explain: Killing the child IS the way the mother is saved; the bad effect is intended as a means, blocking PDE.

questions.q: Aquinas's perfectionism connects:
options: Pleasure with virtue
A thing's essence with its purpose, valuing its perfection in performing that function
Custom with law
Reason with revelation
Right with utility
questions.explain: Teleological worldview: essence + purpose + perfection in function.

questions.q: Value Perfectionism (VP) is best stated as:
options: Things have value only when desired
The goodness of a thing is a matter of that thing fully developing the capacities essential to it
Goodness is reducible to pleasure
Goodness is whatever a culture says
Goodness is identical to virtue
questions.explain: VP grounds value in the development of essential capacities — Aquinas's theory is a version of this.

questions.q: In end-of-life care, NLT typically holds that:
options: Active euthanasia and pain-relieving life-shortening drugs are both wrong
Active euthanasia is wrong, but prescribing a life-shortening drug that EASES the patient's pain can be permissible
Active euthanasia is obligatory in terminal cases
Both are obligatory
Both are optional
questions.explain: Active killing is direct violation; analgesia whose shortening of life is unintended can pass PDE.

questions.q: In self-defence, NLT holds that:
options: Killing the aggressor is forbidden in all cases
Using force is permissible (possibly required); intending the aggressor's DEATH is morally wrong
Force is never permissible
Intending death is permissible if the cause is just
Force is permissible only against animals
questions.explain: Force is permissible; the agent's death must remain a side effect, not the intended end.

questions.q: What is the lecture's verdict on the bystander trolley case vs the footbridge case for the question 'is PDE innate?'
options: Both judged wrong
Both judged permissible
Bystander permissible; footbridge wrong — used as evidence (Harman 1999) that PDE-like principles are built in
Bystander wrong; footbridge permissible
Both judged optional
questions.explain: The contrast in ordinary intuitions tracks the means/side-effect distinction characteristic of PDE.

questions.q: The 'grounding project' for NLT faces what TWO main problems?
options: Vagueness and tolerance
Pleasure and pain
A logical gap between non-moral facts and value (is-ought) AND a circularity in identifying 'good' inclinations
Cultural relativism and atheism
Realism and idealism
questions.explain: You can't validly derive ought from is; and you need a prior conception of human good to flag certain inclinations as good.

questions.q: NLT's response to moral conflict, given commitment to MA, is to:
options: Allow all options as permissible
Use the Principle of Double Effect to distinguish permissible from impermissible cases
Reject MA
Maximise expected utility
Follow the majority
questions.explain: With MA fixed, PDE plays the key role of distinguishing what may and may not be done.

questions.q: Which of the following best captures a CRITICAL difference between the hysterectomy and craniotomy cases?
options: The mother's age
The hospital's protocol
Whether the unborn's death is a side effect (hysterectomy) or a means (craniotomy)
Whether the procedure is publicly funded
Whether the unborn is viable
questions.explain: The means/side-effect distinction is what flips PDE's verdict from permissible to impermissible.

questions.q: Suppose an action satisfies intrinsic permissibility and proportionality but the agent intends the bad effect as the means of bringing about the good. By PDE, the action is:
options: Permissible
Obligatory
Impermissible — failing Non-intentionality alone is sufficient to block PDE
Optional
Permissible only if culturally accepted
questions.explain: All three conditions are JOINTLY required; failing any one blocks PDE.

questions.q: Which is the BEST objection to MA as articulated in the lecture?
options: MA is too easy to apply
MA fits all our intuitions
Cases such as active voluntary euthanasia, assisted suicide, and the Tony Bland case strike many as morally justified yet MA prohibits them
MA is consequentialist
MA cannot accommodate self-defence at all
questions.explain: The pressure on MA comes from cases where intentional killing of an innocent seems morally permissible to reflective people.

questions.q: In Aquinas's argument from natural inclinations, the circularity worry is that:
options: Inclinations themselves are unreal
Not all inclinations are good, so identifying the 'good' inclinations already presupposes a prior account of the human good
Inclinations are private
Inclinations are cultural
Inclinations conflict with reason
questions.explain: The criterion of selection (which inclinations count) presupposes the very thing the inference was supposed to deliver.

questions.q: What does the lecture's appeal to moral PERFECTIONISM imply WITHOUT a commitment to MA and PDE?
options: NLT must reject value entirely
NLT becomes hedonistic
Considerations of value can still come before considerations of rightness, but the central value need not be perfectionistic — welfare/well-being could play that role
NLT collapses into MR
Conflict cases become trivial
questions.explain: Value-first does not entail perfectionism-first; e.g., welfarist consequentialism agrees with the order but disagrees on the content.

questions.q: Why is the hysterectomy case said to satisfy NON-INTENTIONALITY?
options: The unborn's death is the goal of the action
The unborn's death is the means of saving the mother
Neither the END nor the MEANS is the unborn's death — it is a foreseen but unintended side effect of removing the cancerous uterus
No one foresees the death
The mother does not consent
questions.explain: PDE focuses on the agent's intentional structure, not on foreseeability.

questions.q: Two ways the lecture lists by which an effect can be INTENDED are:
options: By desire or by command
As the chosen end of action OR as a means for bringing about some intended end
As a side effect or as a foreseen consequence
By thought or by speech
By utility or by virtue
questions.explain: Both end-intentions and means-intentions count as 'intending' for PDE purposes.

questions.q: Which option correctly distinguishes DIRECT and INDIRECT violation in NLT?
options: Direct = severe; Indirect = mild
Direct = with consent; Indirect = without consent
Both involve hindering a basic value; Direct is NOT PDE-justifiable, Indirect IS
Direct is intentional; Indirect is unintentional, regardless of PDE
Direct violates one good; Indirect violates several
questions.explain: Same physical impact on the basic value, different normative status because of PDE-justifiability.

questions.q: What does the lecture treat as the strongest IMMEDIATE appeal of NLT?
options: Its predictive power
Its mathematical precision
That its principles are grounded in human nature and present themselves as objective, fitting common beliefs about morality
Its egalitarianism
Its tolerance
questions.explain: Grounded objectivity + fit with common moral views is what initially recommends NLT.

questions.q: Suppose a case where the only way to save five lives is to intentionally kill ONE innocent person as the means. By NLT (with MA and PDE), this is:
options: Obligatory because it maximises lives saved
Permissible via PDE
Optional
Impermissible — intentional killing of an innocent is an absolute prohibition, and PDE fails because the death is the means
Permissible if the agent regrets it
questions.explain: Quintessential MA case: the intentional means is precisely what MA forbids; PDE cannot rescue it.

questions.q: Consequentialism's three theses, as stated in the lecture, include:
options: Value is independent of the right
Explanatory priority of value
Personal value alone is relevant
All three of the above
Only (a) and (c)
questions.explain: The three theses listed jointly characterise consequentialism in the lecture.

questions.q: Classical Act Utilitarianism is best characterised as:
options: Rule-based, satisficing, virtue-based, partial, agent-relative
Act-evaluative, maximising, welfarist, universalist, impartialist
Deontological, maximising, welfarist, universalist, impartialist
Act-evaluative, satisficing, hedonist, partial, agent-relative
Rule-based, maximising, welfarist, partial, agent-neutral
questions.explain: Five canonical features: ACT + MAX + WELFARIST + UNIVERSAL + IMPARTIAL.

questions.q: In classical utilitarianism, 'utility' equals:
options: The positive value of effects on those positively affected MINUS the negative value of effects on those negatively affected
The sum of pleasures of the agent only
Whatever the culture says
The probability of success
The intensity of pleasure
questions.explain: Net value across all affected parties.

questions.q: The Generic Principle of Utility states that A in C is right iff:
options: A is required by C's culture
A produces as high a utility as any alternative the agent could perform instead
A is virtuous
A maximises the agent's own pleasure
A is universally accepted
questions.explain: 'At least as high as any alternative' is the maximising test.

questions.q: Bentham's felicific calculus includes which of the following features?
options: Intensity
Duration
Certainty/uncertainty
All of the above
None of the above
questions.explain: Other features: propinquity/remoteness, fecundity, purity, extent.

questions.q: Ethical Hedonism (EH) holds:
options: Pleasure is one good among many
Experiences of pleasure are intrinsically good and pain intrinsically bad; they are the only items of intrinsic value relevant to ethics
Only physical pleasures count
Only mental pleasures count
Pleasure is intrinsically bad
questions.explain: EH is the value theory behind classical hedonistic utilitarianism.

questions.q: PHU (Principle of Hedonistic Utilitarianism) says A in C is right iff:
options: A maximises virtue
A maximises agent's pleasure
Doing A in C would produce at least as high an overall balance of pleasure over pain as any alternative
A is intrinsically pleasant
A is universally accepted
questions.explain: Same maximising structure as the generic PU, with hedonic content for value.

questions.q: Mill refines Bentham by:
options: Rejecting hedonism
Recognising QUALITATIVE differences among pleasures, not just quantitative
Introducing rules
Reducing pleasure to virtue
Rejecting universalism
questions.explain: Quality of pleasure is Mill's signature move within hedonistic utilitarianism.

questions.q: Mill's three-stage 'proof' is supposed to support which conclusion?
options: MR
NLT
That happiness is the only intrinsic good
That virtue is the only good
That utility is unmeasurable
questions.explain: Stages 1–3 build toward the strong claim of happiness as the SOLE intrinsic good.

questions.q: The Subjective Principle of Utility (SPU) uses which logical connective in its formulation?
options: Iff (biconditional)
Either/or
If (one-way conditional)
Neither/nor
Only-if
questions.explain: SPU states a sufficient condition with 'if', not a biconditional — lecture explicitly flags this.

questions.q: Stage 1 of Mill's proof is diagnosed in the lecture as committing:
options: Fallacy of equivocation on 'desirable'
Fallacy of composition
Begging the question
Affirming the consequent
Naturalistic fallacy
questions.explain: Slide from 'able to be desired' to 'worthy of being desired'.

questions.q: Stage 2 of Mill's proof is diagnosed as committing:
options: Fallacy of equivocation
Fallacy of composition
Affirming the consequent
Denying the antecedent
Equivocation on 'happiness'
questions.explain: Inferring properties of the aggregate from properties of the parts (lecture's machine analogy).

questions.q: In the Mine Shafts case, what does Expected-Consequences Utilitarianism (ECU) prescribe?
options: Close gate 1
Close gate 2
Close gate 3 (expected utility 90)
Refuse to act
Flip a coin between gates 1 and 2
questions.explain: Gate 3 has the highest expected value: 0.5·90 + 0.5·90 = 90.

questions.q: After the Mine Shafts decision, when Phil learns the miners were actually in shaft A, Actual-Consequences Utilitarianism (ACU) implies he OUGHT to have:
options: Closed gate 3
Closed gate 1
Closed gate 2
Refused to act
Closed gates 1 and 3
questions.explain: With full knowledge, gate 1 would have saved all 100 — so ACU's verdict is gate 1.

questions.q: Which claim BEST captures the lecture's view of the principle of utility?
options: PU is a step-by-step decision procedure
PU is identical to Mill's proof
PU is a CRITERION of rightness, not a decision procedure — usable as a standard even when not directly applied in deliberation
PU is restricted to one's own culture
PU only applies to omniscient agents
questions.explain: The criterion/decision-procedure distinction is the standard response to the problem of ignorance.

questions.q: Mill's qualitative-hedonism test for ranking pleasures relies on:
options: A scientific instrument
The number of people who experience the pleasure
The preference of those competently acquainted with BOTH pleasures
Brain imaging
The opinion of philosophers
questions.explain: Competent judges' preference is Mill's criterion of quality.

questions.q: Objection 1 to qualitative hedonism is that:
options: Quality cannot be measured at all
Either quality concerns the intrinsic nature of pleasure (collapsing into quantitative hedonism) or it does not (then Mill has abandoned hedonism)
People disagree about quality
Quality is culturally relative
Quality is purely subjective
questions.explain: Dilemma: either back to quantity, or no longer hedonism.

questions.q: What does PHU evaluate to determine rightness?
options: Average pleasure per person
The OVERALL BALANCE of pleasure over pain produced by the action
Only intense pleasures
Only long-lasting pleasures
Only the agent's pleasures
questions.explain: Aggregate of net hedonic effects across all affected.

questions.q: Which of the following describes 'blameless wrongdoing' in the lecture?
options: A right act done for the wrong reason
An objectively wrong act for which the agent is NOT blameworthy because she acted conscientiously on her beliefs
A wrong act done with malice
An optional act done well
A right act done out of luck
questions.explain: Phil in Mine Shafts is the lecture's example — objectively wrong, subjectively right, not blameworthy.

questions.q: On the lecture's two-level account of moral thought, the INTUITIVE level:
options: Is always reliable
Proceeds by moral rules learned early on, applied in ordinary cases
Replaces critical thought
Is restricted to philosophers
Is identical to Bentham's calculus
questions.explain: The critical level engages only when ordinary rules conflict.

questions.q: In Mine Shafts, suppose closing gate 3 saved only 50 miners regardless of location. Expected utilities of all three options are now equal at 50. What does the lecture say about subjective rightness?
options: All three options are subjectively right
Intuitively, closing gate 3 is STILL the subjectively right act, showing SPU's condition is only PRESUMPTIVELY sufficient, not necessary
Closing gate 1 is subjectively right
Closing gate 2 is subjectively right
No option is subjectively right
questions.explain: This is the lecture's example showing SPU offers only a presumptively sufficient condition.

questions.q: Why does the lecture insist that SPU is formulated with 'if', not 'iff'?
options: For literary variety
To match Mill's wording
Because the condition is only PRESUMPTIVELY sufficient and there can be cases where the right answer doesn't match the condition
Because 'iff' is too strong logically in any context
Because SPU is empirically false
questions.explain: Cases like the 50-only-saved variant show the condition is not necessary.

questions.q: The 'parts of happiness' worry directed at Mill's Stage 3 is:
options: That happiness has no parts
That Mill's premises beg the question against virtue
That it is unclear how things desired in and for themselves (e.g., music, health) could be PARTS of happiness rather than independent goods
That happiness varies across cultures
That happiness is unmeasurable
questions.explain: Mill's move to fold independent ends into 'happiness' looks ad hoc.

questions.q: Which best captures the structural relation between ECU and ACU?
options: ACU is a special case of ECU when probability is certain
ECU evaluates by expected outcomes (given evidence); ACU by actual outcomes — they can prescribe DIFFERENT actions in cases of uncertainty
They always agree
ACU presupposes ECU
ECU is identical to PHU
questions.explain: Mine Shafts is the textbook divergence: ECU → gate 3; ACU → gate 1 (given the actual location).

questions.q: Which statement about utilitarian RIGHTNESS and BLAMEWORTHINESS does the lecture endorse?
options: They are identical
Blameworthiness/praiseworthiness ≠ objective wrongness/rightness — they are tied to the SUBJECTIVE notion of moral obligation
Blame depends only on outcome
Praise depends only on intention
Both are determined solely by the agent's culture
questions.explain: Hence blameless wrongdoing AND praiseless rightdoing are coherent possibilities.

questions.q: The strongest internal way for Mill to push back against Objection 1 (the quality dilemma) is to argue that:
options: Quality just reduces to quantity
Quality is irrelevant to ethics
There is a 'middle' notion of quality tied to the intrinsic nature of mental states that is NOT reducible to intensity and duration — though the lecture treats this as still problematic
Hedonism should be abandoned
Pleasures are incomparable
questions.explain: This is Mill's intended escape route, but it remains uncomfortable per the lecture's diagnosis.

questions.q: What is the role of Bentham's felicific calculus in classical hedonistic utilitarianism?
options: A checklist of features (intensity, duration, certainty, propinquity, fecundity, purity, extent) used to calculate utilities of actions
A list of culturally accepted pleasures
A taxonomy of virtues
A rejection of hedonism
A defense of qualitative hedonism
questions.explain: Bentham's calculus operationalises classical utilitarian comparisons of pleasures and pains.

questions.q: Which scenario is BEST handled by ACU rather than ECU?
options: Mine Shafts where action is taken under genuine uncertainty
Retrospective evaluation of an action whose actual consequences are now fully known and clearly differ from expectations
Routine moral deliberation
Decisions under conditions of perfect information
Cases where the agent is uncertain about non-moral facts
questions.explain: ACU's evaluative pull is strongest when we judge in hindsight against actual outcomes.

questions.q: What is the BEST single-sentence summary of the lecture's response to the Problem of Ignorance?
options: Utility is unmeasurable in principle
The principle of utility is a CRITERION of rightness, not a DECISION PROCEDURE — agents need not compute every alternative to be guided by it
PU should be abandoned
Switch to NLT
Adopt ECU and treat it as a procedure
questions.explain: The criterion/procedure distinction allows PU to be normatively authoritative without requiring omniscient calculation.

questions.q: On the lecture's view, which combination of features is unique to CLASSICAL utilitarianism, distinguishing it from NLT and MR?
options: Maximisation + welfarism + universalism + impartialism
Cultural relativism + hedonism
Perfectionism + double effect
Virtue + happiness
Tolerance + pluralism
questions.explain: NLT is perfectionist + absolutist; MR is culturally indexed; classical utilitarianism is precisely max-welfarist-universalist-impartialist.

questions.q: Which case is used in Lecture 6 to illustrate the clash between utilitarianism and retributive justice?
options: A physician sacrificing one patient to save three
Framing an innocent person to prevent a riot
Breaking a promise when circumstances change
A helicopter pilot choosing which group to rescue
Refusing volunteer work to tend a garden
questions.explain: The lecture presents a case where police frame an innocent, isolated individual to prevent a riot. Utilitarianism appears to require this because it maximises overall utility. This clashes with our intuition that punishing the innocent is unjust regardless of consequences.

questions.q: In the medical sacrifice case, what does classical act utilitarianism appear to require the physician to do?
options: Report the patient to authorities
Sacrifice one healthy patient to save three organ-needy patients
Refer all patients to another hospital
Prioritise the patient with the best prognosis
Do nothing and let nature take its course
questions.explain: The physician has a healthy patient whose organs could save three dying patients. Utilitarianism seems to demand sacrificing the one to maximise utility. This clashes with our moral intuition about the right to life.

questions.q: In the distributive justice example, why does utilitarianism prefer Scheme 2 even when it may be objectionable?
options: Because Scheme 2 distributes wealth equally
Because Scheme 2 produces the highest total utility regardless of distribution
Because Scheme 2 respects individual rights
Because Scheme 2 is required by rule consequentialism
Because Scheme 2 fulfils everyone's desires
questions.explain: Utilitarianism is impartialist and maximising: it selects the scheme with the greatest net utility without regard to how that utility is distributed across persons. This can result in ignoring severe inequality.

questions.q: What are the three structural features of classical act utilitarianism identified in Lecture 6?
options: Consequentialist, deontological, and virtue-based
Direct, maximising, and impartialist
Subjective, objective, and relational
Agent-relative, rule-based, and welfare-focused
Hedonistic, pluralist, and preferentialist
questions.explain: The lecture identifies classical utilitarianism as direct (actions evaluated directly for utility), maximising (the right action produces the highest net value), and impartialist (all affected parties given equal weight). These three features together generate the controversial implications.

questions.q: What does 'bold denial' as a utilitarian response mean?
options: Rejecting the principle of impartiality entirely
Accepting the counterintuitive verdict and questioning the reliability of moral intuitions
Replacing maximising with satisficing
Adopting rule consequentialism to avoid counterexamples
Switching to a desire-fulfillment theory of welfare
questions.explain: Bold denial accepts the counterintuitive conclusion (e.g., frame the innocent; sacrifice the one) and challenges whether our moral intuitions are reliable guides. Joshua Greene is cited as arguing that moral intuitions are merely emotional responses dressed up in rational language.

questions.q: What is the 'remote effects' strategy for responding to objections against utilitarianism?
options: Arguing that moral intuitions override empirical evidence
Challenging the stipulated facts by pointing out long-term negative consequences
Replacing the hedonistic theory of welfare with desire fulfillment
Accepting the intuition and abandoning impartiality
Arguing that rule consequentialism handles the case better
questions.explain: The remote effects strategy questions whether the stipulated scenario is empirically realistic. For example, framing an innocent person might undermine public trust in institutions, producing far worse long-run utility. This challenges the premise that framing truly maximises utility.

questions.q: According to de Lazari-Radek and Singer, what must the consistent consequentialist accept regarding the surgeon case?
options: The surgeon should refuse and refer the patient elsewhere
The surgeon should kill the one patient to save four others
The surgeon should seek consent from the patient
The surgeon should apply rule consequentialism instead
The surgeon should act on intuition rather than calculation
questions.explain: De Lazari-Radek and Singer argue that if the surgeon can kill one to save four without detection, the consequentialist must accept this as the right action. They also note the action would not be recommended publicly due to social-order concerns, illustrating the tension between act-level rightness and social recommendation.

questions.q: How does the utilitarian reconcile 'the right thing to do' with 'what would not be recommended' in the surgeon case?
options: By denying that any action could be both right and unrecommended
By distinguishing act-level rightness from what should be publicly advised due to social consequences
By rejecting the case as unrealistic
By switching to rule consequentialism
By adopting desire-fulfillment theory
questions.explain: The utilitarian can say the action is right at the act level but should not be recommended because public knowledge of such behaviour would damage social order, producing negative utility. This separates the criterion of rightness from the decision procedure.

questions.q: What is satisficing consequentialism (SC)?
options: An action is right iff it produces the single best outcome
An action is right iff its consequences meet or exceed a specified utility threshold
An action is right iff it follows an ideal code of rules
An action is right iff it fulfils the agent's desires
An action is right iff it promotes human flourishing
questions.explain: SC replaces 'maximising' with 'satisficing': an action is permissible if its utility meets or exceeds a threshold, even if an alternative would produce higher utility. This is meant to accommodate personal projects and partiality.

questions.q: Which case is used to show that satisficing consequentialism has unacceptable implications involving theft?
options: The helicopter rescue case
Collecting charity donations but keeping some for oneself when the remainder still exceeds the threshold
The medical sacrifice case
Breaking a promise to a dying man's daughter
The gardening vs. volunteering case
questions.explain: If a charity collector keeps some donated money yet the remaining donation still exceeds the threshold utility, SC says this is permissible. But this effectively permits theft, which is an unacceptable implication of the view.

questions.q: In the helicopter rescue case, what unacceptable implication does satisficing consequentialism generate?
options: Ian must always save the larger group
Ian does nothing wrong if he saves the 2 instead of the 6, as long as the outcome exceeds the threshold
Ian must sacrifice some campers to save others
Ian is required to maximise utility by saving the 6
Ian must flip a coin to be impartial
questions.explain: SC implies Ian does nothing wrong saving 2 rather than 6 provided the outcome meets the threshold. This violates our intuition that saving fewer lives when you could save more — at no extra cost — is morally wrong.

questions.q: What is rule consequentialism (RC)?
options: Each action is evaluated directly by its consequences
Actions are right if permitted by an ideal code of rules whose general acceptance would maximise utility
Actions are right if they satisfice a utility threshold
Actions are right if they fulfil agent preferences
Actions are right if they accord with natural law
questions.explain: RC evaluates an ideal code of rules by the utility produced if individuals internalised those rules. An action is right if it is not prohibited by that ideal code, allowing the theory to accommodate intuitions about promising, medical ethics, and demandingness.

questions.q: How does rule consequentialism handle the demandingness objection?
options: By requiring strict impartiality at all times
By arguing that internalising a rule of strict impartiality would be too costly given human psychology, so a less demanding rule would have higher acceptance utility
By adopting a desire-fulfillment theory of welfare
By using bold denial to question moral intuitions
By switching to satisficing consequentialism
questions.explain: RC notes that humans are deeply disposed toward partiality. The costs of internalising a rule of strict impartiality would outweigh the benefits, so an ideal code would include rules permitting some partiality — making the theory less demanding than act utilitarianism.

questions.q: What is the 'collapsing objection' against rule consequentialism?
options: RC collapses into natural law theory when applied consistently
When rules are made complex enough to handle all exceptions, RC becomes extensionally equivalent to act consequentialism
RC collapses into satisficing consequentialism
RC collapses into ethical egoism when agent utility is prioritised
RC becomes indistinguishable from Kantian ethics under ideal conditions
questions.explain: The worry is that a sufficiently complex rule (e.g., 'keep promises unless breaking them produces greater value') ends up coinciding with act consequentialism in every case. This undermines RC's claim to be a distinct theory.

questions.q: What is the purpose of Nozick's experience machine thought experiment in Lecture 6?
options: To defend hedonism as the correct theory of welfare
To challenge hedonism by showing that what matters to us goes beyond subjective experiences
To support rule consequentialism over act consequentialism
To illustrate the demandingness objection
To defend satisficing consequentialism
questions.explain: Nozick asks whether we would plug into a machine that simulates any desired experience. Most people would not, suggesting that what makes life good is not merely subjective experience (contra hedonism) but also actually doing things, being a certain person, and engaging with reality.

questions.q: What is desire fulfillment consequentialism?
options: An action is right iff it maximises subjective pleasure
An action is right iff it maximises the fulfillment of actual desires across all affected parties
An action is right iff it follows an ideal rule code
An action is right iff it promotes human flourishing
An action is right iff it meets a utility threshold
questions.explain: Desire fulfillment consequentialism replaces hedonic welfare with desire fulfillment. An action is right if it produces the most general desire fulfillment. This responds to the experience machine objection by moving beyond mere subjective experience.

questions.q: Which objection applies specifically to desire fulfillment consequentialism?
options: It is too demanding
Not all fulfilled desires contribute to welfare, and fulfilling horrendous desires does not seem intrinsically valuable
It collapses into act consequentialism
It ignores the distribution of welfare
It cannot handle the experience machine case
questions.explain: Two problems arise: (1) some fulfilled desires (e.g., a desire to count blades of grass) do not plausibly benefit the person; (2) fulfilling morally monstrous desires (e.g., sadistic desires) seems to be of no intrinsic value, which the theory cannot easily explain.

questions.q: What is perfectionist consequentialism?
options: A view that actions are right if they produce the most pleasure
A view that certain states make life good without necessarily contributing to welfare or happiness, linked to natural law
A view that rules aimed at moral perfection are binding
A view that desire fulfillment is the highest good
A view that satisficing a threshold is morally sufficient
questions.explain: Perfectionist consequentialism holds that certain objective states (e.g., achievement, knowledge, friendship) make a life good independent of subjective welfare or pleasure. The lecture notes this brings act consequentialism closer to Aquinas's natural law theory.

questions.q: In the promising case, what is the utilitarian temptation and what does it illustrate?
options: The utilitarian must keep the promise regardless of circumstances
The utilitarian is tempted to break the promise and keep the money when the daughter is now rich and the agent is poor, illustrating the clash with the moral intuition that promises must be kept
The utilitarian must maximise the daughter's happiness
The utilitarian should follow rule consequentialism to handle this case
The utilitarian should adopt satisficing to avoid the implication
questions.explain: The case involves money held in trust for a daughter who is now wealthy, while the agent has fallen into poverty. Utilitarianism is tempted to endorse breaking the promise (keeping the money yields more utility). This conflicts with the intuition that promises generate binding obligations independent of consequences.

questions.q: What does the demandingness objection claim about classical utilitarianism?
options: It is not demanding enough because it allows partiality
It demands too much by requiring the agent to always choose the action with the greatest overall utility, even at great personal cost
It is incompatible with rule-following
It demands maximisation in only a narrow set of cases
It is self-defeating because universal maximisation reduces total utility
questions.explain: Classical utilitarianism requires the impartial maximisation of utility, which can demand that agents sacrifice personal projects, relationships, and comfort whenever doing so would benefit others more. The gardening vs. volunteering example illustrates this: the agent is required to volunteer whenever that produces more utility.

questions.q: What structural feature does rule consequentialism reject compared to classical act utilitarianism?
options: Welfarism
The direct feature — rightness is no longer determined by the consequences of individual actions but by compliance with rules
Impartialism
Consequentialism entirely
The maximising feature at the level of individual actions (though it retains it at the level of rule-selection)
questions.explain: RC retains maximising at the level of selecting ideal rules (the ideal code maximises utility if internalised), but at the level of individual actions, rightness is determined by rule-compliance, not direct consequence calculation. This makes it indirect rather than direct.

questions.q: What makes the stipulation in the punishment case philosophically problematic according to the remote effects strategy?
options: It is logically self-contradictory
It assumes that framing the innocent really does maximise utility, but this ignores realistic long-term consequences such as erosion of institutional trust
It ignores the agent's self-interest
It conflates rule and act consequentialism
It assumes hedonism is the correct theory of value
questions.explain: The remote effects strategy argues the stipulation that framing the innocent maximises utility is empirically unrealistic. In practice, such behaviour risks public discovery, undermining institutional trust and producing catastrophic long-term disutility.

questions.q: Which philosopher is cited in Lecture 6 as challenging the reliability of moral intuitions?
options: Peter Singer
Joshua Greene
Robert Nozick
Katarzyna de Lazari-Radek
John Stuart Mill
questions.explain: Joshua Greene is cited as arguing that strong moral feelings ('some things simply cannot be done') are not obviously rational — they are emotional responses that philosophers dress up with rational-sounding stories. This supports the bold denial strategy of questioning intuitions.

questions.q: What is the key difference between 'direct' and 'indirect' consequentialism?
options: Direct consequentialism evaluates rules; indirect evaluates actions
Direct consequentialism evaluates actions by their own consequences; indirect evaluates actions by their conformity to rules or dispositions
Direct consequentialism is hedonistic; indirect is preferentialist
Direct consequentialism is impartialist; indirect is agent-relative
Direct consequentialism uses a threshold; indirect uses maximising
questions.explain: In direct (act) consequentialism, each individual action is assessed by its consequences. In indirect versions (e.g., rule consequentialism), actions are assessed by whether they conform to rules or dispositions that, if adopted, would produce the best consequences.

questions.q: In the special obligations case, what does utilitarianism appear to require regarding the agent's son versus a neighbour's more promising student?
options: The agent must split money equally between both students
The agent is obligated to fund the neighbour's more promising student if doing so produces better consequences
The agent has no obligation to fund either student
The agent must fund their own child regardless of consequences
The agent must donate to charity instead of funding any individual student
questions.explain: Utilitarianism's impartiality requires the agent to use money where it produces the greatest utility. If the neighbour's child would benefit more, the agent is obligated to fund that child over their own son, conflicting with our intuitions about special parental obligations.

questions.q: Which of the following is NOT one of the responses to objections against utilitarianism listed in Lecture 6?
options: Rebut the objections directly (bold denial or remote effects)
Defend an alternative theory of welfare
Reject one or more structural features of classical utilitarianism
Adopt Kantian deontology as a supplement
Propose satisficing or rule consequentialism
questions.explain: The lecture lists three types of response: rebutting objections directly, defending an alternative theory of welfare, and rejecting structural features. Adopting Kantian deontology is not listed as a utilitarian response — it would represent abandoning consequentialism altogether.

questions.q: What does it mean for two moral theories to be 'extensionally equivalent'?
options: They have the same foundational premises
They agree on the deontic status (right/wrong/optional) of every action in every possible case
They use the same definition of welfare
They are derived from the same normative principles
They produce the same distribution of utility
questions.explain: Two theories are extensionally equivalent when they give the same verdict (obligatory, wrong, or optional) for every action in every circumstance, even if they justify those verdicts differently. The worry is that a maximally complex version of rule consequentialism agrees with act consequentialism on every case.

questions.q: What is the 'distributive objection' to utilitarianism?
options: Utilitarianism ignores overall utility
Utilitarianism allows the worse distribution of utility to be preferred if total utility is higher, ignoring how welfare is spread across persons
Utilitarianism demands too much of individual agents
Utilitarianism cannot handle cases of promising
Utilitarianism collapses into rule consequentialism
questions.explain: The distributive objection holds that utilitarianism is indifferent to distribution: a scheme that concentrates high utility in a few while leaving others below the poverty line can be preferred to a more equal scheme with lower total utility. This conflicts with our sense of fairness and justice.

questions.q: What role does 'acceptance utility' play in rule consequentialism?
options: It measures the utility of each individual action
It measures the utility produced if a given rule were internalised and followed by members of society
It refers to the threshold utility in satisficing consequentialism
It is the utility of the agent accepting a personal sacrifice
It measures desire fulfillment across all rational agents
questions.explain: Acceptance utility is the overall level of utility that would result if individuals internalised (accepted and acted on) a given rule. RC selects the ideal code by comparing acceptance utilities of alternative rule-sets.

questions.q: What distinguishes non-welfarist from welfarist versions of consequentialism?
options: Non-welfarist versions deny that consequences matter
Non-welfarist versions hold that what makes outcomes good is not exclusively welfare or happiness but other objective values like achievement or knowledge
Non-welfarist versions are rule-based rather than act-based
Non-welfarist versions reject the maximising feature
Non-welfarist versions are identical to natural law theory
questions.explain: Welfarist consequentialism holds that only welfare (pleasure, desire fulfillment) contributes to the value of outcomes. Non-welfarist versions (like perfectionist consequentialism) allow that objective goods such as knowledge, achievement, or virtue have intrinsic value independent of their contribution to welfare.

questions.q: Which feature distinguishes ethical egoism from classical utilitarianism?
options: Ethical egoism is consequentialist; utilitarianism is not
Ethical egoism is agent-relative (only the agent's own utility counts); utilitarianism is impartialist
Ethical egoism is maximising; utilitarianism satisfices
Ethical egoism is welfarist; utilitarianism is not
Ethical egoism uses rules; utilitarianism evaluates individual actions
questions.explain: Both theories are consequentialist and maximising. The key difference is that ethical egoism is agent-relative: only the agent's own utility determines rightness. Utilitarianism is impartialist and counts everyone's utility equally.

questions.q: According to the theory of right conduct in Lecture 7, when is an action obligatory under ethical egoism?
options: When it maximises total utility
When it would produce higher agent utility than any alternative
When it fulfils the agent's most immediate desire
When it is permitted by an ideal rule code
When it accords with the agent's rational self-interest in a satisficing sense
questions.explain: EE holds that an action is obligatory for P iff P's performing it would produce higher agent utility than any other available action. 'Agent utility' refers to utility accruing specifically to the agent P.

questions.q: What is the difference between ethical egoism (EE) and the definition of an optional action under EE?
options: They are identical

Optional actions are those where at least one alternative produces at least as much agent utility as the chosen action, while obligatory actions are those producing strictly more utility than all alternatives
EE applies to obligatory actions only; optional actions are outside its scope
Optional actions require no calculation; obligatory actions do
EE covers only obligatory actions; optional actions are covered by a separate theory
questions.explain: The definition of EE (right action produces at least as much agent utility as alternatives) superficially resembles the definition of an optional action. The difference: obligatory actions produce strictly more utility than all alternatives; optional actions are those where the agent is indifferent between two or more equally good options.

questions.q: What is subjective ethical egoism (SEE)?
options: An action is right iff it maximises the agent's objective self-interest
An action is right iff the agent believes it would produce at least as much agent utility as alternatives
An action is right iff it satisfies the agent's desires
An action is right iff it is rational given the agent's preferences
An action is right iff it accords with the agent's long-term happiness
questions.explain: SEE is a subjective variant where rightness is determined by what the agent believes will maximise their utility, not by what actually does. This contrasts with objectivist EE where rightness tracks actual consequences.

questions.q: What question does rational egoism (RE) address, and how does it differ from EE?
options: RE asks what is morally right; EE asks what is rational
RE asks what is rational; EE asks what is morally right — they address different normative questions
RE is a descriptive theory; EE is normative
RE covers group rationality; EE covers individual morality
RE and EE are identical
questions.explain: RE addresses the rationality of actions: an action is rationally permissible iff it maximises agent utility. EE addresses the morality of actions. They are 'cousins' because a common assumption is that it is always rational to do what is morally right, making them closely related but answering different questions.

questions.q: Why is EE said to 'compete with any theory that denies all morally right action is necessarily self-interested'?
options: Because EE denies that morality exists
Because EE claims that the only morally right actions are self-interested ones, directly contradicting theories that require impartial or altruistic action
Because EE is purely descriptive
Because EE defines rationality in terms of morality
Because EE is a subtype of utilitarianism
questions.explain: EE's core claim is that only self-interested actions can be morally right. This places it in direct competition with utilitarianism, Kantian ethics, and any other theory that demands impartiality or other-regarding action.

questions.q: What does psychological egoism (PE) claim?
options: All agents ought to act in their self-interest
Any deliberate action an agent performs is ultimately motivated by that agent's self-interest
All agents maximise their own pleasure
Altruistic motivations are always irrational
Self-interest and morality coincide in all cases
questions.explain: PE is a descriptive, empirical thesis: it claims that in fact every deliberate human action has self-interest as its ultimate motivating force. It is not a normative claim about what agents ought to do.

questions.q: How does psychological egoism differ from psychological hedonism?
options: They are identical
PE allows ultimate motives beyond pleasure (e.g., power, reputation); psychological hedonism restricts ultimate motives to pleasure
PE is normative; psychological hedonism is descriptive
PE applies only to deliberate actions; hedonism applies to all behaviour
PE requires conscious awareness of self-interest; hedonism does not
questions.explain: Psychological hedonism is a narrower claim: the ultimate motive is always pleasure. PE is broader: the ultimate motive is always self-interest, which may include things other than pleasure such as power, fame, or legacy.

questions.q: What is the argument from PE to EE in Lecture 7?
options: PE is normative; therefore EE is descriptive
Since 'ought' implies 'can', and PE says all motivated actions serve self-interest, any obligatory action must be self-interested — supporting EE
PE shows altruism is irrational, therefore EE is correct
PE demonstrates that desire fulfillment equals moral rightness
PE's empirical evidence directly entails EE as a normative conclusion
questions.explain: The argument: (1) ought implies can; (2) PE says all deliberate actions are ultimately self-interested; therefore (3) any obligatory action must be one the agent can perform — which PE says is ultimately self-interested — so all obligatory actions maximise self-interest (EE).

questions.q: What is the 'aversive-empathic arousal reduction' explanation for helping behaviour under PE?
options: Helping others produces direct pleasure in the agent
Helping is motivated by a desire to reduce one's own distress at witnessing another's suffering
Helping is motivated by social rewards
Helping is driven by a desire for self-punishment avoidance
Helping is driven by rational self-interest calculation
questions.explain: Under PE, helping someone in distress is explained as reducing the helper's own empathic discomfort. The ultimate motive is self-interested (eliminating one's own aversive emotional state), not genuinely altruistic.

questions.q: What did Batson's experiments test, and what did they find?
options: Whether people help more when rewards are offered — they found rewards increase helping
Whether people who could escape witnessing suffering without helping would still help — they found many did, suggesting genuine altruism
Whether self-punishment avoidance explains all helping behaviour — they confirmed it
Whether social esteem drives all prosocial behaviour — they confirmed it
Whether PE predicts economic behaviour correctly — they found it does
questions.explain: Batson designed experiments where participants could escape witnessing suffering without helping. PE predicts they would avoid helping if they could avoid the aversive experience. Many participants helped anyway, suggesting that genuine altruistic motivation (benefiting the other) may exist — contra PE.

questions.q: What is the 'What You Most Want' argument for PE, and why does it fail?
options: It argues agents always maximise expected utility — it fails because utility is subjective
It argues that since agents do what they most want, they act from self-interest — it fails because 'wanting' in the explanatory sense does not require the object of desire to be self-interested
It argues that pleasure is the ultimate motive — it fails because some people act from pain
It argues that rational action is always self-interested — it fails empirically
It argues that all desires reduce to self-preservation — it fails in cases of self-sacrifice
questions.explain: The argument conflates two senses of 'want': the liking sense (feeling attraction) and the explanatory sense (having a motivating state). In the explanatory sense, acting on a desire to benefit another person is not self-interested just because it is what the agent wants — the object of the desire is the other's welfare.

questions.q: What is the 'Satisfaction Argument' for PE, and what is its flaw?
options: It argues that satisfaction is always physical pleasure — flaw: satisfaction can be non-physical

It argues that since desire fulfillment produces positive feeling, all actions are ultimately motivated by that positive feeling (self-interest) — flaw: it confuses the object of desire with the effect of fulfillment
It argues that rational agents always maximise satisfaction — flaw: they sometimes satisfice
It argues that PE is entailed by evolutionary biology — flaw: evolution can select for altruism
It argues that self-interest is the highest good — flaw: this is a normative not descriptive claim
questions.explain: The Satisfaction Argument infers from 'fulfilling a desire produces a positive feeling' to 'the motive was to produce that feeling'. This confuses the object of desire (the other's welfare) with the by-product of fulfillment (the agent's satisfaction). Having a desire directed at another's benefit is not the same as being motivated by one's own satisfaction.

questions.q: What is the Principle of Non-Interference (PNI) and why does it create a consistency problem for EE?
options: PNI says agents should never interfere in others' affairs; EE supports this
PNI says it is wrong to interfere with someone performing their duty; EE may require interference whenever it serves the agent's self-interest, creating a potential contradiction
PNI is an EE principle; no tension exists
PNI applies only to non-egoistic theories
PNI says interference is always permissible if self-interested
questions.explain: PNI holds that it is wrong to interfere with someone performing their duty. EE requires doing whatever maximises agent utility, which may include interfering with another's dutiful action. The lecture notes this apparent conflict but suggests it is resolvable by qualifying PNI.

questions.q: How does EE handle the problem of determinacy under uncertainty, according to the lecture?
options: EE has no response to uncertainty
EE can adopt the same strategy as some utilitarians: operate on expected agent utility under uncertain conditions
EE requires perfect information to generate verdicts
EE switches to rule-based reasoning under uncertainty
EE defers to conventional morality under uncertainty
questions.explain: Like utilitarianism, EE can respond to uncertainty by operating on expected agent utility — the probability-weighted sum of possible outcomes for the agent. This mirrors how some utilitarians handle empirical uncertainty about consequences.

questions.q: What does 'external support' mean as a criterion for evaluating moral theories?
options: A theory should be supported by religious authorities
A theory should be consistent with well-established theories from other fields such as biology or psychology
A theory should be externally verifiable through surveys
A theory should have support from legal systems
A theory should be endorsed by a majority of philosophers
questions.explain: External support means a moral theory should be consistent with (ideally supported by) findings from other well-established disciplines. For EE, this raises the question of whether biology and psychology support or undermine the claim that self-interested motivation is universal.

questions.q: Why does Batson's empirical evidence not straightforwardly refute EE?
options: Because EE is unfalsifiable
Because EE is a normative theory (about what one ought to do) and PA (psychological altruism) is a descriptive theory (about what motivates action) — they operate at different levels
Because Batson's experimental design was flawed
Because EE can incorporate altruistic motives
Because PE is not relevant to EE
questions.explain: EE is a normative theory: it says agents ought to maximise their self-interest. PA (psychological altruism) claims that agents sometimes are motivated by others' welfare. These are independent claims — showing that altruistic motivation exists does not show that agents ought to act altruistically.

questions.q: What is 'psychological altruism' (PA)?
options: The view that agents should act for others' benefit
The descriptive claim that human agents can and sometimes do act from the ultimate motive of benefitting others
The view that altruistic motivation is always rational
The view that self-interest and altruism always coincide
The normative claim that altruism is morally required
questions.explain: PA is the empirical, descriptive counterpart to PE. It holds that at least some human actions are genuinely motivated by an ultimate desire to benefit others, not merely as an instrumental means to self-interested ends.

questions.q: What distinguishes 'ultimate' from 'non-ultimate' motives under PE?
options: Ultimate motives are conscious; non-ultimate are unconscious
Ultimate motives are the final end an action serves; non-ultimate motives serve as means to some further end
Ultimate motives are stronger; non-ultimate are weaker
Ultimate motives are rational; non-ultimate are emotional
Ultimate motives are innate; non-ultimate are learned
questions.explain: PE holds that self-interest is always the ultimate motive — the final goal the action is aimed at. Other motives (e.g., helping a friend) can exist as non-ultimate, instrumental means to the ultimate self-interested goal. What counts for PE is the final, irreducible aim.

questions.q: The lecture states EE could adopt the 'critical vs intuitive' distinction. What does this mean?
options: Critical morality overrides intuitive morality in all cases
In everyday contexts agents use intuitive rules; in reflective contexts agents engage in critical calculation — EE can operate at both levels
Critical reasoning always validates self-interest
Intuitive morality is always egoistic
The distinction applies only to utilitarianism
questions.explain: Some utilitarians (e.g., Hare) distinguish critical from intuitive moral reasoning. EE can similarly hold that agents normally operate on intuitive rules of thumb aligned with self-interest, while engaging in explicit self-interest calculation only in critical reflective contexts.

questions.q: Which of the following is the correct formal statement of EE as given in Lecture 7?
options: An action A by P in C is right iff P performing A maximises total utility
An action A by P in C is right iff P performing A would produce at least as much agent utility as any other action P might perform instead
An action A by P in C is right iff P believes A maximises agent utility
An action A by P in C is right iff A follows an ideal agent-relative rule code
An action A by P in C is right iff A satisfices agent utility above a threshold
questions.explain: This is the formal definition given in the lecture: EE holds that A is right (not wrong) iff performing A produces at least as much agent utility as any alternative. This captures the maximising, agent-relative character of the theory.

questions.q: Why is EE described as 'agent-relative' (or agent-centered)?
options: Because it requires agents to calculate utility relative to the average person
Because rightness is determined solely by consequences for the acting agent, not by consequences for all affected parties
Because it is sensitive to the agent's cultural context
Because the agent must weigh their own interests against others proportionally
Because it is indexed to the agent's subjective beliefs
questions.explain: EE is agent-relative because the moral status of an action depends entirely on how it affects the agent performing it. The welfare of others has no direct moral weight; only the agent's own utility determines rightness.

questions.q: What is meant by EE being 'prudentialist'?
options: It holds that prudence (caution) is the cardinal virtue
It holds that what is good for the agent (their welfare or self-interest) is what determines moral rightness
It holds that agents must be cautious in calculating expected utility
It holds that moral rightness requires long-term planning
It holds that self-interest and social interest coincide in the long run
questions.explain: 'Prudentialist' in this context means that the theory grounds moral rightness in what is good for (prudentially in the interest of) the acting agent. The good life for the agent — their self-interest or welfare — is the measure of rightness.

questions.q: Can PE (psychological egoism) be used to directly derive EE (ethical egoism) as a normative claim?
options: Yes, because descriptive facts always entail normative conclusions
No, because deriving a normative conclusion (EE) from a purely descriptive premise (PE) commits the is-ought fallacy
Yes, because PE shows agents are capable of self-interested action
No, because PE is false
Yes, because rational self-interest is both descriptive and normative
questions.explain: The is-ought problem (Hume's guillotine) shows that normative conclusions cannot be derived from purely descriptive premises without a bridging normative premise. PE alone, as a descriptive claim, cannot directly entail EE as a normative claim.

questions.q: What is one pro-egoistic explanation for helping behaviour involving 'social esteem'?
options: Helping produces intrinsic pleasure independently of recognition
Helping is motivated by a desire for social approval or improved self-image — an ultimately self-interested motive
Helping is driven by a genuine concern for the other's welfare
Helping reduces the agent's empathic arousal
Helping is driven by fear of legal punishment
questions.explain: The social esteem explanation holds that agents help others because doing so enhances their reputation, social standing, or self-image. These are self-interested benefits, so helping remains compatible with PE even when it appears altruistic.

questions.q: Why does EE have a problem with the Principle of Non-Interference specifically?
options: EE requires always following conventional moral rules

EE requires the agent to do whatever maximises their self-interest, which may involve interfering with another agent pursuing their EE duty — creating a system that is self-undermining or internally inconsistent
EE forbids all interference as a matter of principle
EE cannot generate duties of any kind
EE requires complete isolation from other agents
questions.explain: If EE says each agent must maximise their self-interest, and one agent's self-interest requires blocking another agent from doing their EE duty, the theory may generate conflicts where agents are simultaneously required to act and forbidden from having their actions blocked. This threatens coherence.

questions.q: What is 'self-punishment avoidance' as a PE explanation for helping?
options: Agents help to avoid legal punishment
Agents help to avoid the guilt, shame, or self-blame they would experience if they failed to help — a self-interested motive
Agents help because punishment from others is likely
Agents help to build a reputation for avoiding self-blame
Agents help because helping is a habit requiring no conscious motivation
questions.explain: Self-punishment avoidance explains helping as motivated by avoiding negative self-directed emotions (guilt, shame) that would arise from not helping. Since avoiding these feelings is in the agent's self-interest, the behaviour is compatible with PE.

questions.q: What does the lecture mean when it says PE 'refers to deliberate actions'?
options: PE applies only to premeditated criminal acts
PE only covers actions that are intentional and chosen, not reflexive or automatic behaviours
PE covers all physical movements of a human body
PE applies only to actions with foreseeable consequences
PE applies only to rational agents with full information
questions.explain: PE is a thesis about the ultimate motivation behind deliberate, intentional choices. It does not make claims about reflexive responses or purely automatic behaviour. This scoping is important for its empirical testability.
