questions.q: Which of the following best describes the relationship between EE and rational egoism (RE)?
options: They are identical theories with different names
RE addresses rational permissibility; EE addresses moral rightness — they are related because morality is commonly assumed to coincide with rationality, making RE relevant to the defence of EE
RE is a descriptive theory; EE is empirical
RE is a stronger claim than EE
EE entails RE but RE does not entail EE
questions.explain: RE and EE are distinct but related. RE concerns rationality (what is rational to do); EE concerns morality (what is right to do). Given the common assumption that rational action and moral action coincide, RE provides indirect support for EE.

questions.q: Which of the following is NOT listed in Lecture 7 as a pro-egoistic explanation for helping behaviour?
options: Aversive-empathic arousal reduction
Self-punishment avoidance
Social esteem / self-rewards attraction
Genuine concern for the other person's welfare
None of the above — all are listed as pro-egoistic
questions.explain: Genuine concern for the other person's welfare is the altruistic explanation, not a pro-egoistic one. The three pro-egoistic explanations listed are: aversive-empathic arousal reduction, self-punishment avoidance, and social esteem/self-rewards attraction.

questions.q: According to Kant, where must the ground of moral obligation be sought?
options: In the natural desires and inclinations of human beings
In concepts of pure reason, not in facts about human nature
In the consequences of actions for overall welfare
In social contracts between rational agents
In religious authority and divine command
questions.explain: Kant argues that a moral law must hold with absolute necessity for all rational beings. Therefore its ground cannot be in contingent facts about human nature but must be found in pure reason alone.

questions.q: What is the difference between a categorical imperative (CI) and a hypothetical imperative (HI)?
options: CI applies only to humans; HI applies to all rational beings
CI is unconditionally binding regardless of one's ends; HI is conditionally binding depending on one's ends
CI concerns happiness; HI concerns duty
CI is about actions; HI is about intentions
CI is empirical; HI is a priori
questions.explain: A CI commands unconditionally ('Do this!') regardless of what the agent wants. A HI is conditional ('Do A if you want E'). Kant argues morality must be categorical because moral obligations do not depend on an agent's contingent desires.

questions.q: Why can morality NOT be a system of hypothetical imperatives according to Kant?
options: Because HIs are too demanding
Because moral duty requires action regardless of the ends one happens to adopt; a system of HIs would make morality contingent on desires
Because HIs apply to all rational beings, not just humans
Because HIs are derived from pure reason
Because HIs are incompatible with freedom
questions.explain: Moral duty involves obligations that must be met regardless of the agent's desires. HIs are only binding given certain ends. If morality were a system of HIs, one could escape moral obligations by giving up the relevant ends.

questions.q: What is an 'end in itself' in Kantian ethics?
options: A goal that is valued instrumentally as a means to further ends
Something with unconditional, non-instrumental value that must be respected for its own sake
A consequentialist conception of value
A desire that the agent has adopted through rational reflection
A natural good identified by practical reason
questions.explain: An end in itself has unconditional value: it cannot be merely used as an instrument. Kant holds that rational nature (humanity) is the paradigmatic end in itself, grounding the Formula of Humanity.

questions.q: What is 'autonomy' in the Kantian sense and why is it the ground of dignity?
options: The freedom to pursue any desired end without restriction
The capacity of rational agents to give themselves the moral law through reason, grounding their unconditional worth
The ability to maximise one's welfare independently
The psychological independence from social influence
The ability to deliberate about means to given ends
questions.explain: Kantian autonomy is self-legislation: rational agents are the source of the moral law for themselves through practical reason. This capacity for self-determination gives persons unconditional worth (dignity) that must be respected.

questions.q: State the Formula of Humanity (FH) as given in Lecture 8.
options: Act only on maxims you could will as universal laws of nature
Act so that you use humanity, in your own person and in others, always as an end, never merely as a means
Always maximise the welfare of all rational beings
Treat all persons equally regardless of the consequences
Do whatever best promotes the development of rational nature
questions.explain: FH: 'So act that you use humanity, in your own person as well as in the person of any other, always at the same time as an end, never merely as a means.' (G, 4:429) It has both a negative part (do not treat persons merely as means) and a positive part (promote certain ends).

questions.q: What is the difference between treating someone as a means and treating them merely as a means?
options: There is no difference; both are equally wrong
Treating someone as a means is permissible if they consent; treating them merely as a means is wrong because you regard them as nothing more than a tool with no independent value
Treating as a means is voluntary; merely as a means is involuntary
Treating as a means concerns actions; merely as a means concerns attitudes
Treating as a means is permissible only for self-regarding actions
questions.explain: In everyday transactions (e.g., asking a friend for a lift), others serve as means but they retain their full status as ends — their consent and interests matter. The mugger treats the victim as merely a means: the victim's autonomous will is completely subordinated to the mugger's ends.

questions.q: Does 'regarding someone as a mere means' automatically constitute 'treating them as a mere means' according to the lecture?
options: Yes, attitude and action are identical in Kantian ethics
No — the gangster who regards the coffee seller as a vending machine still acts permissibly in buying coffee; it is the action, not merely the attitude, that must not treat persons merely as means
Yes, but only for perfect duties
No, but attitudes are still morally evaluable
Yes, because Kant focuses entirely on inner states
questions.explain: The lecture cites the gangster case: his attitude is morally objectionable but the transaction (buying coffee) is not itself wrong because he does not act in a way that treats the seller as merely a means. FH primarily constrains actions, though attitudes are also relevant.

questions.q: What is the positive requirement of FH, beyond the negative prohibition on treating persons merely as means?
options: To maximise others' welfare
To promote self-perfection and the happiness of others
To follow rules derived from the Formula of Universal Law
To treat all persons with equal respect
To develop one's talents to the greatest extent possible
questions.explain: FH's positive requirement is to 'harmonize with humanity' in others. Kant specifies two positive duties: self-perfection (developing one's powers and talents) and promoting the happiness of others.

questions.q: What is the distinction between perfect and imperfect duties in Kant's system?
options: Perfect duties are more important than imperfect ones
Perfect duties must always be performed (or never performed) to the fullest extent; imperfect duties must be performed sometimes and to some extent but allow latitude
Perfect duties apply to all rational beings; imperfect duties apply only to humans
Perfect duties concern others; imperfect duties concern oneself
Perfect duties are derived from FH; imperfect duties from FUL
questions.explain: A perfect duty admits of no exception: for example, one must never lie. An imperfect duty (e.g., beneficence) must be fulfilled but the agent has latitude in when and how — it cannot be completely ignored but can be expressed in various ways.

questions.q: State the Formula of Universal Law (FUL) as given in Lecture 8.
options: Act so as to produce the greatest utility for all rational beings
Act only according to that maxim through which you can at the same time will that it become a universal law
Treat humanity always as an end and never merely as means
Act on maxims that could be accepted by all affected parties
Always perform the action that a fully rational being would perform
questions.explain: FUL: 'Act only according to that maxim through which you can at the same time will that it become a universal law.' (G, 4:421) It functions as a decision procedure, unlike FH which is a fundamental criterion of rightness.

questions.q: What is a 'maxim' in Kantian ethics?
options: An objective principle of morality derived from pure reason
A subjective principle of volition on which an agent acts whenever she acts intentionally
A universal rule binding on all rational beings
A hypothetical imperative concerning means to ends
A rule derived from empirical facts about human nature
questions.explain: A maxim is a subjective principle of volition: the rule the agent in fact acts on. Its general semantic form is 'I will do X, when/if Y, in order to Z.' The universalizability test asks whether this subjective rule can be consistently willed as a universal law.

questions.q: What is the Formula of the Law of Nature (FLN)?
options: Act so that your actions accord with the laws of physical nature
Act as if the maxim of your action were to become by your will a universal law of nature
Act in accordance with natural law as identified by reason
Act only on maxims that could be part of a rational social contract
Act so that rational beings always flourish
questions.explain: FLN is a variant of FUL: 'Act as if the maxim of your action were to become by your will a universal law of nature.' Together FUL/FLN constitute the universalizability test: a maxim is universalizable iff it can be consistently willed as a law of nature.

questions.q: What is the 'false promising' example meant to illustrate about FUL?
options: That keeping promises always produces better consequences
That a maxim of making false promises when in need cannot be universalised because it generates a contradiction: universal false-promising destroys the institution of promising
That promise-keeping is a perfect duty derivable only from FH
That false promising is wrong because it treats the lender merely as a means
That the satisficing version of consequentialism handles this case better
questions.explain: If everyone made false promises whenever in need, the institution of promising would collapse — no one would believe promises. The maxim of false promising is therefore not universalizable: willing it as universal law contradicts itself by destroying the very practice the maxim presupposes.

questions.q: In the selfishness example, why does the maxim fail FLN even without a logical contradiction?
options: Because selfishness is intrinsically irrational
Because willing universal selfishness conflicts with a maxim of rationality: the agent wills to be helped when in need, which is inconsistent with willing that no one helps anyone in need
Because selfish maxims always fail universalisation
Because self-interest is agent-relative and cannot be universalised
Because FLN requires perfect impartiality
questions.explain: A maxim of selfishness ('never help others') can be imagined as a law of nature without logical contradiction. But Kant argues it still fails because we cannot rationally will it as universal: we simultaneously will to receive help when we need it, creating a practical contradiction.

questions.q: What is the 'false negative' objection to FUL?
options: FUL sometimes wrongly permits actions that are actually wrong
FUL sometimes wrongly prohibits actions that are not morally wrong — e.g., a maxim about withdrawing savings when the stock market rises cannot be universalised but is not morally wrong
FUL fails to generate duties in cases of clear moral wrongdoing
FUL prohibits all self-interested actions
FUL is unable to handle the false-promising case
questions.explain: A false negative arises when FUL says an action is wrong (because its maxim is not universalizable) but intuition says the action is permissible. The stock-market withdrawal example: this maxim may fail universalisation but is not a moral matter.

questions.q: What is the 'false positive' objection to FUL?
options: FUL sometimes correctly permits what should be wrong
FUL sometimes wrongly permits actions that are morally wrong — e.g., a hyper-specific maxim about deception on a particular date can be universalised but the action is intuitively wrong
FUL generates too many duties
FUL fails to distinguish perfect from imperfect duties
FUL cannot generate the false-promising case correctly
questions.explain: A false positive arises when FUL says an action is permissible (maxim is universalizable) but the action is actually wrong. A maxim so specific (e.g., 'I will deceive Igor Cycz on March 8 to buy a metal detector') may be universalizable yet intuitively wrong.

questions.q: What is 'moral worth' according to Kant in Lecture 8?
options: The value of an action's consequences for overall welfare
The disposition to act from the sole motive of duty (good will), combined with the inner strength to resist temptation
The intrinsic value of rational nature
The value of virtuous character independent of consequences
The satisfaction produced by fulfilling one's duties
questions.explain: Moral worth for Kant requires: (1) a good will — being disposed to act from duty as the sole motive when duty calls; and (2) the inner strength to consistently act from this good will despite temptation. Actions done from inclination alone, even if correct, lack moral worth.

questions.q: What is the 'highest good' (summum bonum) in Kantian ethics?
options: Maximum total utility across all rational beings
The combination of virtuous character and happiness proportionate to that virtue
Pure practical reason exercised without empirical constraint
The unconditional freedom of rational will
The development of all of humanity's natural capacities
questions.explain: The highest good has two components: virtue (moral worthiness to be happy) and happiness befitting one's virtue. Kant cites the 'impartial rational spectator': it would be wrong to take delight in the prosperity of someone without a good will.

questions.q: What is 'immortality as a practical postulate' in Kantian ethics?
options: Kant believes humans can prove the soul is immortal through reason

Because the highest good requires perfect virtue, and perfect virtue requires endless progress, the immortality of the soul is a postulate of practical reason — not theoretically knowable but required for moral coherence
Kant holds that only immortal beings can be subject to moral law
Immortality ensures that happiness always follows virtue in the long run
Kant argues that fear of death motivates moral behaviour
questions.explain: Kant argues that achieving perfect virtue requires endless moral progress, which is only possible if the soul continues indefinitely. Immortality is thus a 'postulate' — something we must assume for the moral law to be coherent — even though we cannot theoretically cognise it.

questions.q: Why does Kant posit God's existence as a practical postulate?
options: To provide a divine lawgiver for moral commands
Because the highest good requires a cause adequate to ensure that happiness is proportionate to virtue, and only God can guarantee this coordination
To ground the categorical imperative in divine authority
To explain why rational beings have dignity
To derive natural law from divine reason
questions.explain: The highest good requires that virtue be rewarded with proportionate happiness. Nothing in the natural world guarantees this. Kant therefore postulates God as a cause capable of ensuring the correspondence between virtue and happiness — not as theoretically provable but as a practical necessity.

questions.q: What is 'casuistry' in Kant's system of duties?
options: A method for calculating expected utility in complex cases
A procedure for applying general moral principles to specific cases, required when moving from abstract principles to concrete judgments
A type of moral relativism that adjusts duties to cultural context
The study of duties to oneself only
A method for resolving conflicts between FH and FUL
questions.explain: Kant emphasises that a metaphysics of morals needs principles of application. Casuistry is the procedure of moving from universal principles (Level 3) to particular verdicts about specific actions (Level 4), requiring judgment about how principles apply in concrete circumstances.

questions.q: In 'Lizbeth's lie', how does Kant resolve the apparent conflict between the duty not to lie and the duty to protect innocent life?
options: By concluding that lying is always impermissible regardless of consequences
By applying 'the stronger ground of obligation prevails' — the duty to protect innocent life outweighs the duty of honesty in this case
By concluding both duties are merely imperfect
By using FUL to show that lying to protect life is universalizable
By concluding that Lizbeth has no duty in this situation
questions.explain: Kant's principle is 'the stronger ground of obligation prevails.' In Lizbeth's case, the threat to Tom's life creates a duty of greater strength than the duty of strict honesty, so lying is permitted. This is an application of casuistry resolving a genuine conflict of duties.

questions.q: According to Kant, how does rational nature connect to the capacity to set ends?
options: Rational nature consists in maximising one's natural ends
The capacity to set oneself any end whatsoever characterises humanity; this capacity requires developing certain powers, hence duties of self-perfection
Rational nature consists in following rules derived from universal law
The capacity to set ends is purely instrumental and has no moral significance
Rational nature is grounded in physical constitution, not end-setting capacity
questions.explain: Kant holds that the capacity to set any end — the ability to choose one's own goals — characterises humanity. Developing this capacity requires cultivating natural talents and powers, generating imperfect duties of self-perfection as part of the positive requirement of FH.

questions.q: Why does Kant say morality cannot be a doctrine of personal happiness?
options: Because happiness is impossible for most people
Because happiness is empirically contingent and subjective, while moral duty is unconditional and a priori
Because happiness is an end adopted based on desire, making morality hypothetical
Both B and C — happiness-based morality would reduce to hypothetical imperatives dependent on desires
Because happiness conflicts with the highest good
questions.explain: Kant argues that if morality were aimed at personal happiness, moral obligations would be hypothetical: 'Do this if you want to be happy.' But happiness is something people happen to want contingently; moral duty must hold regardless of what one wants. Happiness-based morality collapses into HIs.

questions.q: What does FH require regarding 'self-perfection' as a positive duty?
options: Perfecting one's moral character to achieve sainthood
Developing one's natural talents and powers since the capacity for rational agency requires their cultivation
Maximising happiness in one's own life
Developing only the talents directly required for performing perfect duties
Pursuing higher education as a universal requirement
questions.explain: Self-perfection is a positive, imperfect duty under FH. Because the capacity for rational agency requires the exercise and development of natural powers, agents have an obligation to cultivate their talents and capacities — though they have latitude in how they do so.

questions.q: What role does 'practical reason' play in Kantian ethics according to Lecture 8?
options: It calculates expected utility for moral decisions
It is the capacity to deliberate and make free choices, grounding both the categorical imperative and the dignity of persons
It provides empirical knowledge about the consequences of actions
It is the faculty of moral sentiment that produces feelings of obligation
It derives moral rules from facts about human flourishing
questions.explain: Practical reason is the faculty of deliberation and free choice. For Kant, it is the source of the categorical imperative (via the Formula of Universal Law and Formula of Humanity) and the ground of human dignity, since autonomy — self-legislation through practical reason — is what gives persons unconditional worth.

questions.q: How does Kant's universalizability test differ from rule consequentialism?
options: They are identical
The universalizability test asks whether a maxim can be consistently willed as universal law (logical/practical consistency); RC asks whether following a rule maximises utility — different criteria
Kant's test is consequentialist; RC is deontological
Kant's test applies to individual actions; RC applies to whole institutions
Kant's test requires empirical calculation; RC is purely a priori
questions.explain: The lecture explicitly notes the universalizability test is not rule consequentialism. FUL tests for logical/practical consistency: can this maxim be universalised without contradiction? RC tests for utility maximisation. These criteria can diverge: a universalizable maxim may have low utility; a high-utility rule may fail universalisation.

questions.q: Under the theory of right conduct based on FH, when is an action optional?
options: When it produces the maximum possible agent utility
When neither performing nor omitting the action fails to respect humanity
When it is permitted by the ideal code of rules
When its maxim is universalizable under FUL
When it satisfices the welfare threshold
questions.explain: Under the FH formulation: an action is obligatory if omitting it would fail to respect humanity; it is wrong if performing it fails to respect humanity; it is optional if neither performing nor omitting fails to respect humanity (i.e., FH does not determine the matter either way).

questions.q: What does Kant mean when he says the non-human rational beings (if they exist) are also bound by the moral law?
options: That animals have moral rights
That moral law has universal scope across all rational beings, not just humans — grounded in reason, not in facts about human nature specifically
That alien beings must follow the same empirical moral customs as humans
That rational nature is distributed across multiple species
That the moral law is a law of nature applying to all living things
questions.explain: Kant opens by noting the command 'do not lie' does not apply just to humans — any rational being is bound by it. This underscores that moral law is grounded in pure reason (which any rational being exercises), not in contingent features of humanity.

lectures.title: Lecture 10: Virtue Ethics and Care Ethics

questions.q: Which kind of moral theory takes virtue as central to understanding morality?
options: Consequentialist theories
Virtue ethics
Deontological theories
Care ethics only
Social contract theories
questions.explain: Virtue ethics places virtuous character traits and virtuous persons at the centre of moral evaluation, treating facts about right conduct as derivative from facts about what a virtuous agent would do.

questions.q: What are the three components of a virtue according to Lecture 10, using honesty as the example?
options: Rational, social, and behavioural
Intellectual (correct beliefs about when honesty is appropriate), affective (disposed to feel approval of honest behaviour), and motivational-behavioural (disposed to tell the truth)
Cognitive, emotional, and volitional
Dispositional, habitual, and conscious
Natural, cultivated, and expressed
questions.explain: The lecture identifies three components: the intellectual component (correct beliefs), the affective component (appropriate emotional responses and attitudes), and the motivational-behavioural component (disposition to act accordingly). All three together constitute a virtue.

questions.q: How are virtues acquired according to Aristotle (and the lecture)?
options: They are naturally endowed at birth
They are acquired by practice and exercise — one becomes courageous by doing courageous acts
They are derived from rational insight alone
They are given by divine grace
They are acquired through theoretical study of ethics
questions.explain: Aristotle distinguishes virtues from natural endowments: virtues are stable character traits built through repeated practice. One becomes virtuous by performing virtuous actions habitually until those dispositions are fixed.

questions.q: What are the Greek cardinal virtues mentioned in the lecture?
options: Faith, hope, love, and prudence
Wisdom, courage, temperance, and justice
Honesty, generosity, compassion, and integrity
Prudence, fortitude, temperance, and justice
Wisdom, justice, piety, and moderation
questions.explain: The lecture lists the Greek cardinal virtues as wisdom, courage, temperance, and justice. These are 'cardinal' because other virtues depend on them; they are not subordinate to any other virtue.

questions.q: What is the 'Function Argument' in Aristotle's Nicomachean Ethics?
options: The highest good is whatever the majority of people pursue
The function of humans is rational activity; since the good of a thing is performing its function well, the human good is rational activity guided by virtue — eudaimonia
The function of human society is to maximise overall welfare
Humans have no distinctive function; the good is subjective
The highest good is pleasure because pleasure is what all actions aim at
questions.explain: Aristotle argues: all activities aim at some good; the highest good is most choice-worthy; the human function is the exercise of reason (unique to humans); the good of a thing is performing its function well; therefore the human good (eudaimonia) is rational activity of the soul guided by virtue.

questions.q: How does 'eudaimonia' differ from ordinary 'happiness'?
options: Eudaimonia is purely subjective; ordinary happiness is objective
Eudaimonia is a moralized, objective concept requiring virtue; ordinary happiness is subjectively determined and does not require living virtuously
Eudaimonia applies to all living things; ordinary happiness applies only to humans
Eudaimonia is identical to pleasure; happiness is broader
Eudaimonia requires external goods; ordinary happiness does not
questions.explain: Unlike ordinary happiness (which depends on how one feels), eudaimonia is value-laden: one cannot be eudaimon without living virtuously. This also distinguishes Aristotle's view from the Stoics: Aristotle holds virtue is necessary but not sufficient for eudaimonia; the Stoics hold virtue is both necessary and sufficient.

questions.q: What is the Doctrine of the Mean?
options: Virtue requires always choosing the mathematically average action
Virtue is the disposition to aim at the intermediate between excess and deficiency in feelings and actions relative to us
Virtue requires moderation in all circumstances without exception
Virtue is the mean between two virtues
Virtue requires minimising all emotional responses
questions.explain: Aristotle holds that virtue concerns feelings and actions where both excess and deficiency are errors. Virtue aims at the intermediate — not a fixed numerical midpoint but what is appropriate for the person in the circumstances. E.g., courage is the mean between cowardice (deficiency) and recklessness (excess).

questions.q: What is phronesis (practical intelligence) and why is it a higher-order virtue?
options: It is the virtue of theoretical wisdom about mathematical truths
It is the capacity to determine the right manner of feeling and action in particular circumstances calling for virtuous response; it is higher-order because it guides all other virtues
It is the virtue of following established moral rules
It is the virtue of maximising overall utility
It is a natural endowment rather than an acquired virtue
questions.explain: Phronesis is practical wisdom: the ability to perceive the morally salient features of a situation and determine the right response. It is higher-order because without it, other virtues may be misdirected. It comes only with life experience — a morally mature adult has it; a nice child does not yet.

questions.q: How does the Unity of Virtues thesis work according to the lecture's example?
options: All virtues are reducible to a single virtue
Being truly just requires also being temperate, because without temperance one would take more than one's fair share — virtues support and require each other in a complete character
Each virtue is independent and can be possessed without the others
Unity of virtues means virtues cannot conflict
Unity of virtues is a claim about the logical entailment of virtue definitions
questions.explain: The lecture's example: in rationing, justice requires taking only a fair share, but unless one is also temperate, one will be disposed to take more. Being truly just therefore requires temperance. The virtues are mutually reinforcing and a fully virtuous person must possess them together.

questions.q: State the formal definition of Virtue Ethics (VE) given in Lecture 10.
options: An action is right iff it produces the best consequences for the agent

An action A in C is obligatory iff any ideally virtuous agent would not fail to do A in C; wrong iff no ideally virtuous agent would perform A in C; optional iff some ideally virtuous agent might or might not
An action is right iff it accords with the virtue of the agent performing it
An action is right iff it is recommended by at least one virtuous person
An action is right iff a consensus of virtuous agents would endorse it
questions.explain: VE uses a 'qualified agent' standard: right action is what an ideally virtuous agent (possessing all morally significant virtues and consistently acting on them) would do. Obligatory = what any such agent would not omit; wrong = what no such agent would perform; optional = underdetermined.

questions.q: What is the 'mendacious person' counterexample to VE, and what does it show?
options: A person who lies shows that VE permits dishonesty

A compulsive liar who decides to reform cannot just decide to be virtuous; he must engage in a regimen of concrete steps — but these steps are not what an ideally virtuous agent would do, revealing that VE's standard fails to guide moral improvement
The counterexample shows VE is too demanding
The counterexample shows VE permits lying under certain conditions
The counterexample shows the unity of virtues is false
questions.explain: The person trying to overcome dishonesty must take concrete practical steps (tracking lies, rewarding honesty) that a virtuous person would never need. VE's standard — what would an ideally virtuous agent do? — cannot guide this person's path to virtue, showing a limitation in VE's action-guiding capacity.

questions.q: What is the 'obstinate person' modification to VE, and what counterexample does it generate?
options: VE is modified so that what matters is what a virtuous agent would advise; but a virtuous agent might strategically advise the obstinate person to NOT do the right thing — which would mean VE says the wrong thing is obligatory
VE is modified to require absolute obedience to virtuous advisors
The modification resolves all problems with VE's action-guidance
The modification shows that VE collapses into care ethics
The modification shows VE reduces to rule consequentialism
questions.explain: The modified VE says an action is obligatory iff any ideally virtuous agent would advise one to do it. But an obstinate person does the opposite of any advice. A virtuous agent, knowing this, would strategically advise the wrong action to prompt the right one — so the modified theory says the obligatory action is the one the virtuous agent falsely advises.

questions.q: What is the central inspiration for care ethics?
options: Aristotle's Nicomachean Ethics
The psychologist Carol Gilligan's 1982 book In a Different Voice, which argued for a distinct care-oriented approach to moral reasoning often associated with women's moral experience
Kant's Groundwork for the Metaphysics of Morals
The natural law tradition
Nozick's experience machine thought experiment
questions.explain: Care ethics was inspired by Carol Gilligan's empirical and theoretical work challenging Kohlberg's justice-centered model of moral development. Gilligan argued for a care orientation in moral reasoning, one rooted in relationships rather than abstract principles.

questions.q: What is the 'justice vs care' contrast in terms of the conception of self?
options: Justice sees the self as relational; care sees the self as individual
Justice sees the self as individual; care sees the self as constituted by relationships with others
Justice and care share the same conception of self
Justice sees the self as social; care sees the self as natural
Justice sees the self as rational; care sees the self as emotional
questions.explain: The justice perspective conceives of persons as primarily individual, separate agents; the care perspective sees persons as constituted by their relationships. This difference in metaphysics of the self underlies the different approaches to moral problems.

questions.q: What is the distinction between 'caring about' and 'caring for' in care ethics?
options: Caring about means being indifferent; caring for means active intervention
Caring about means being invested in something or someone (an attitude); caring for means the active provision of care (actions and practices)
Caring about applies to strangers; caring for applies only to intimate relationships
Caring about is a virtue; caring for is a duty
They are synonymous in care ethics
questions.explain: 'Caring about' is the attitudinal sense: being invested in another's wellbeing. 'Caring for' refers to the active, practical dimension: actually doing things to meet another's needs. Both dimensions are important in care ethics.

questions.q: What is Joan Tronto's first stage of care ('Attentiveness') according to Lecture 10?
options: Ensuring that care actually meets the need
Recognising that a need exists and overcoming moral blindness to it
Moving from 'someone should act' to 'I must act'
Considering the care-receiver's perspective
Recognising care as a collective responsibility
questions.explain: Tronto's first stage is 'Caring About': attentiveness to others' needs. This requires noticing that a need exists in the first place — overcoming the moral blindness that allows us to ignore others' suffering.

questions.q: What is Tronto's fifth stage of care?
options: Care-giving (competence)
Solidarity (Caring With) — recognising that care is a collective responsibility of citizenship, not just an individual or private matter
Responsiveness (Care-Receiving)
Responsibility (Taking Care Of)
Attentiveness (Caring About)
questions.explain: Tronto's fifth stage, 'Caring With' (solidarity), extends care ethics beyond interpersonal relationships to the political level: care is a collective responsibility that citizens share and must organise socially and politically.

questions.q: According to Stephanie Collins, what is care ethics NOT a theory of?
options: Personal relationships
The whole of morality — it does not cover all moral duties (e.g., duties not to harm, promise-keeping) but specifically duties that arise from relationships of dependency
Virtuous character
Political obligations
Global justice
questions.explain: Collins argues care ethics addresses a specific domain of morality: duties arising from relationships of care and dependency. It does not replace other moral considerations such as duties not to harm or duties from promise-making. It is complementary to, not a replacement for, broader moral theory.

questions.q: State the 'dependency principle' from care ethics as given in Lecture 10.
options: If a relationship of care exists, both parties share equal duties
When an important interest is unfulfilled and you are capable of fulfilling it at non-excessive cost, you have a responsibility to fulfill it
You must care for anyone who depends on you regardless of cost
Care is owed only in existing personal relationships
Care responsibilities are unlimited and can never be outweighed
questions.explain: Collins's dependency principle: when an important interest (material or non-material) is unfulfilled, you are capable of fulfilling it, and doing so will not be too costly, you have a responsibility to fulfil it. This grounds care responsibilities without requiring pre-existing personal relationships.

questions.q: What is the 'principle of subsidiarity' in care ethics?
options: Care should be distributed equally across all social levels
Care should be delivered at the most local and personal level possible; we should enable people to care for themselves wherever possible
Care is primarily a state responsibility
Care should flow from the most powerful to the most vulnerable
Care responsibilities diminish with social distance
questions.explain: Engster's principle of subsidiarity: actual care delivery should be shifted to the most local/personal level where possible; care should enable self-care and autonomy rather than creating dependency. This balances the responsive provision of care with respect for the care-receiver's agency.

questions.q: What makes care ethics distinct in its view of 'moral reasoning' compared to the justice perspective?
options: Care ethics uses formal logical inference; justice uses intuition
Care ethics emphasises sensitivity to particular relationships and context; justice emphasises impartial universal rules
Care ethics is rule-based; justice is case-based
Care ethics prioritises rights; justice prioritises welfare
Care ethics is derived from Kantian ethics; justice comes from virtue ethics
questions.explain: The justice perspective uses impartial, universal reasoning. Care ethics emphasises emotional attunement, sensitivity to particular relationships, and responsiveness to the specific needs of those one is in relationship with — a more particularistic and context-sensitive mode of moral reasoning.

questions.q: Why is care ethics not necessarily restricted to existing personal relationships?
options: Because care ethics requires caring for all sentient beings equally

Advocates argue that care responsibilities can extend globally through the dependency principle — one can have responsibilities to distant strangers whose important interests one could fulfill at reasonable cost
Because care ethics has no principled limit on who counts
Because all humans are in relationships with each other
Because care ethics is indistinguishable from cosmopolitan ethics
questions.explain: While care ethics originated from close personal relationships, proponents argue it need not be limited to them. The dependency principle grounds responsibilities to anyone whose important interests we can fulfill at reasonable cost, extending care ethics toward a global scope.

questions.q: For Aristotle, why is virtue necessary but not sufficient for eudaimonia?
options: Because eudaimonia also requires fame and power
Because eudaimonia also requires external goods (health, friends, sufficient resources) and not just virtuous inner character
Because eudaimonia requires divine favour in addition to virtue
Because eudaimonia requires perfect virtue, which no human achieves
Because eudaimonia is purely subjective and depends on feelings of happiness
questions.explain: Aristotle holds that virtuous character is essential for eudaimonia but not sufficient: a life of virtue also needs external goods to flourish. The Stoics disagree — for them, virtue alone is both necessary and sufficient for the eudaimon life.

questions.q: What is Tronto's 'Responsiveness' stage of care?
options: Moving from awareness to action
Considering the care-receiver's perspective and being alert to potential abuses of power in the caring relationship
Ensuring that care is competently delivered
Recognising care as a collective political responsibility
Taking responsibility for ensuring care occurs
questions.explain: Responsiveness (Care-Receiving) involves attending to how the care-receiver experiences and responds to care, and remaining alert to power imbalances. Care relationships can be sites of dependence and potential exploitation; responsiveness guards against this.

questions.q: What does VE say about moral rules?
options: VE endorses a fixed set of exceptionless moral rules derived from virtues
VE generates virtue rules from each virtue (e.g., 'be kind') but these can conflict when multiple virtues apply, so rules are not foundational
VE rejects all moral rules as incompatible with virtue
VE holds that rules are derivable from a single master virtue
VE holds that rules must be followed regardless of character
questions.explain: VE can derive virtue rules from individual virtues, but since VE features multiple cardinal virtues, conflicts arise when two virtue rules indicate incompatible actions. VE therefore cannot treat rules as foundational; phronesis is needed to navigate conflicts.

questions.q: In the VE formal definition, what distinguishes 'obligatory' from 'optional' actions?
options: Obligatory actions maximise welfare; optional actions satisfice
Any ideally virtuous agent would not fail to do an obligatory action; for optional actions, some ideally virtuous agent might or might not perform it — there is no single verdict
Obligatory actions accord with justice; optional actions accord with benevolence
Obligatory actions are perfect duties; optional actions are imperfect
Obligatory actions promote eudaimonia; optional actions are neutral
questions.explain: VE holds an action is obligatory iff any ideally virtuous agent would not omit it — the virtuous verdict is unanimous and it must be done. An action is optional when the virtuous verdict is indeterminate — some ideally virtuous agents might do it and some might not.

questions.q: What is the 'Responsibility' (Taking Care Of) stage in Tronto's framework?
options: Recognising a need exists
Moving from 'someone should do something' to 'I/We must ensure this is done' — taking on the responsibility for care
Ensuring care is competently delivered
Attending to the care-receiver's response
Recognising care as a political collective responsibility
questions.explain: The Responsibility stage involves assuming ownership of addressing a recognised need. It is the shift from passive awareness ('someone should help') to active commitment ('I or we must ensure this happens').

questions.q: What does the lecture say about forms of care that 'stifle development'?
options: All forms of care are equally morally valuable
Not all forms of relationship and care are equally morally valuable — care that stifles the development or autonomy of the person cared for is problematic
Stifling care is only a problem in political, not personal, relationships
Stifling care is morally neutral if well-intentioned
Stifling care is only a concern when it involves children
questions.explain: The lecture explicitly notes that not all forms of care are equally morally valuable. Care that prevents the growth or undermines the autonomy of the care-receiver is morally problematic, even if well-intentioned. This connects to the principle of subsidiarity's emphasis on enabling self-care.

questions.q: According to Aristotle, virtues can be hierarchically arranged with 'cardinal virtues' at the top. What makes them cardinal?
options: They are the most frequently practised virtues
They are not dependent on other virtues and other virtues are subordinate to them
They were first identified by Aristotle himself
They require the most natural talent to develop
They are the virtues associated with political life
questions.explain: Cardinal virtues are those that are not derivative of other virtues — they stand independently at the top of a hierarchy. Other virtues depend on or are subordinate to them. This distinguishes cardinal from secondary virtues.

questions.q: What does the 'Competence' (Care-Giving) stage in Tronto's framework require?
options: That care is offered regardless of whether it meets the need
That the care provided actually meets the need — it is not enough to intend to help; care must be effective
That the caregiver has professional qualifications
That care is organised at the highest institutional level
That care-receivers consent explicitly to all care given
questions.explain: Competence in care-giving requires that care actually addresses and meets the need in question. Good intentions and taking responsibility are insufficient; the care must be delivered effectively and skillfully.

questions.q: What is the central contrast between moral generalism and moral particularism?
options: Generalism holds morality is subjective; particularism holds it is objective
Generalism holds morality depends on principles; particularism holds it depends on the morally relevant features of particular cases which may not behave in a fixed principle-like way
Generalism holds intuitions are unreliable; particularism trusts intuitions
Generalism applies to rules; particularism applies to virtues
Generalism is consequentialist; particularism is deontological
questions.explain: The central contrast: generalism holds that principles are fundamental to morality (explaining and guiding right action); particularism holds that moral features of particular cases are primary, and these features may vary in their moral contribution across contexts rather than behaving in fixed principle-like ways.

questions.q: What is 'Principle Eliminativism' (PE) in moral particularism?
options: The view that all moral principles should be simplified
The view that there are no correct moral principles needed to explain or represent the deontic status of particular actions
The view that agents should eliminate principles from their decision-making process
The view that absolute principles are all false but contributory principles remain valid
The view that PE and PA always go together
questions.explain: Principle Eliminativism holds that no moral principles are true or correct — there are no general facts about morality that principles accurately represent. This is a metaphysical/semantic thesis about the existence of moral principles.

questions.q: What is 'Principle Abstinence' (PA) in moral particularism?
options: A metaphysical claim that no principles are true
A practical claim that a morally committed agent seeking correct verdicts should not rely solely on principles for guidance
The view that all moral generalizations are false
The view that principles should be abstracted from cases
A form of moral scepticism
questions.explain: PA is a practical/methodological thesis: even if some principles were true, an agent who relies on them risks misapplying them or having attention distorted. Correct moral judgment requires sustained attention to the particulars of each case, not mechanical principle-application.

questions.q: What are 'absolute principles' vs 'contributory principles' in moral theory?
options: Absolute principles apply everywhere; contributory principles apply only in some contexts
Absolute principles say a feature always generates a fixed, decisive verdict; contributory principles say a feature counts for or against but can be outweighed
Absolute principles are true; contributory principles are only rules of thumb
Absolute principles concern perfect duties; contributory principles concern imperfect duties
Absolute principles come from generalism; contributory principles from particularism
questions.explain: An absolute principle is exceptionless: once the specified feature is present, the verdict is fixed and cannot be overridden. A contributory principle says the feature has moral weight but that weight is defeasible — it can be outweighed by other moral considerations.

questions.q: What are the three theses that constitute 'The Generalist Picture' according to Lecture 11?
options: Direct, maximising, and impartialist

Generic Generalist Thesis (small set of fundamental moral features explain right/wrong), Universal Relevance Thesis (morally relevant features are relevant wherever they appear), and Polarity Thesis (features that count in favour always count in favour)
Absolute principles, contributory principles, and moral reasons
Reasons atomism, reasons holism, and double defeasibility
Principle eliminativism, principle abstinence, and moral discernment
questions.explain: The generalist picture rests on: (1) GGT — a small set of fundamental features explain rightness; (2) URT — relevant features stay relevant across contexts; (3) PT — features that count positively always count positively. These three together constitute a strong generalist position.

questions.q: What is 'reasons atomism'?
options: The view that moral reasons are irreducibly particular
The view that the moral contribution of a feature is fixed independently of context — a feature with positive moral charge retains that charge across all situations
The view that moral reasons can only be understood in isolation from one another
The view that only one fundamental moral reason exists
The view that moral reasons cannot be weighed against each other
questions.explain: Reasons atomism holds that the contribution of a feature is intrinsic to it: if it counts in favour in one case, it always counts in favour in any case. Context does not alter its valence, only potentially outweigh it. Atomists allow reasons to outweigh but not reverse each other.

questions.q: What is 'reasons holism'?
options: The view that all moral reasons must be considered together in a single judgment
The view that the moral contribution of a feature depends on context — a feature that is a reason in one case may be no reason or even a reason in the opposite direction in another
The view that moral reasons are always reducible to a single master reason
The view that moral reasons accumulate additively
The view that context determines which principles apply but not what they say
questions.explain: Reasons holism (also called 'variability of reasons') holds that the same feature can count for an action in one context, count against it in another, or be morally irrelevant in a third. Context holistically determines the contribution of any feature.

questions.q: What is 'reversal' as used in moral particularism?
options: When a moral reason is outweighed by a stronger reason
When a feature's moral polarity changes across contexts — what counts in favour in one case counts against in another — distinct from merely being outweighed
When a principle generates a verdict opposite to intuition
When an agent reverses a previous moral decision
When a duty is reversed by a stronger duty
questions.explain: Reversal is specifically when a feature changes its moral contribution from positive to negative (or vice versa) — not merely outweighed. In the public execution example: pleasure might count against reintroducing executions (due to sadistic context), not merely be outweighed by cruelty.

questions.q: What is McNaughton's example used to illustrate reversal?
options: A promise made under duress

A government considering reintroducing public executions: the fact that spectators would enjoy the spectacle might count against (not just be outweighed by) reintroduction because pleasure here takes on a negative moral valence
A doctor withholding truth from a patient
A lie told to protect someone from violence
A case of self-defence that also benefits an aggressor
questions.explain: McNaughton shows that whether pleasure counts as a reason for or against depends on context. In the case of public executions, sadistic pleasure might actually be a reason against — not just outweighed — illustrating genuine reversal rather than mere outweighing.

questions.q: How does 'reversal' differ from 'outweighing' in particularist terminology?
options: Reversal occurs when a stronger reason cancels a weaker one; outweighing occurs when a feature changes polarity

In outweighing, a positive feature retains its positive charge but is overcome by stronger considerations; in reversal, the feature itself changes from positive to negative (or vice versa) in this context
They are synonymous in particularist terminology
Reversal applies only to absolute principles; outweighing applies to contributory ones
Outweighing changes polarity; reversal preserves polarity
questions.explain: The key distinction: outweighing means a feature counts positively but is defeated by stronger countervailing considerations (it still contributes positively, just insufficiently). Reversal means the feature itself contributes negatively here — its moral sign has flipped.

questions.q: What is 'silencing' in moral particularism?
options: When a moral argument is too complex to evaluate
When a feature that usually functions as a moral reason becomes morally irrelevant in a particular context
When an agent is unable to speak about a moral matter
When a strong duty silences a weaker competing duty
When consensus silences minority moral views
questions.explain: Silencing occurs when a feature that normally gives a reason becomes entirely morally irrelevant in a particular context. Example: a promise to help someone cheat on an exam — the fact of having promised is silenced and gives no reason to help the cheating.

questions.q: What is the 'Generalist Strategy' of 'Atomistic Specification' in response to particularist counterexamples?
options: Replace simple principles with a small set of holistic principles
Add conditions to principles whenever they face counterexamples, making them more complex to avoid the problem case
Replace principles with virtue rules
Accept particularism but retain moral generalizations
Argue that all apparent counterexamples involve reasoning errors
questions.explain: Atomistic specification: when a simple principle (e.g., 'keep promises') faces a counterexample, add the conditions needed to avoid it (e.g., 'keep promises unless coerced, unless the promised act is immoral, unless the promise is cancelled...'). This makes the principle more complex but aims to preserve its validity.

questions.q: What is the particularist's reply to atomistic specification using the concepts of 'reasons', 'enablers', and 'disablers'?
options: The particularist accepts that the conditions make the principle true

The conditions added are not additional reasons — they are enablers (allowing the promise to function as a reason) or disablers (preventing it from functioning as a reason), not themselves reasons. The specified principle conflates reasons with enabling/disabling conditions
The conditions show that only holistic principles are possible
The conditions prove that reasons atomism is correct
The conditions make the principle extensionally equivalent to act consequentialism
questions.explain: The particularist distinguishes reasons (considerations that count for or against) from enablers/disablers (conditions that determine whether a consideration functions as a reason at all). Conditions like 'the promise was not coerced' are not themselves reasons — it would be odd to say 'I should go because my promise wasn't coerced.' They enable or disable the promise from functioning as a reason.

questions.q: What is 'Holistic Accommodation' as a generalist strategy?
options: Abandoning reasons holism to maintain simple principles
Accepting reasons holism while insisting that moral principles are still possible and useful — giving rise to holistic generalism
Accepting particularism but retaining some principles as heuristics
Replacing absolute with contributory principles only
Using case comparison instead of principles to derive verdicts
questions.explain: Holistic accommodation accepts the particularist point about reasons holism but resists principle eliminativism. The response is holistic generalism: principles can be formulated to incorporate holistic sensitivity by including a 'holism clause' and an 'overriding clause'.

questions.q: What is a 'holistic principle' as defined in Lecture 11?
options: A principle that applies only in holistic contexts

Feature F counts in a certain way when the surrounding context allows it to do so, and when no contextual factor silences, reverses, disables, or outweighs it — incorporating both a holism clause and an overriding clause
A principle that considers all relevant features simultaneously
A principle that applies differently to different cultures
A principle derived by considering the totality of an agent's character
questions.explain: A holistic principle has three components: a morally significant feature (e.g., 'this action kills a rational agent'), a holism clause (acknowledging potential silencing/reversal/disabling), and an overriding clause (allowing for the feature to be outweighed). This is called 'double defeasibility'.

questions.q: Why does holistic generalism (HG) say that reasons holism (RH) does not entail principle eliminativism (PE)?
options: Because RH and PE address different moral questions
Because principles can be reformulated with holism clauses to accommodate variable contributions of features — RH undermines atomistic principles but not holistic ones
Because PE requires accepting PA, not RH
Because RH is about action-guidance, not metaphysics
Because holistic principles are not really principles at all
questions.explain: HG shows that even if every feature's moral contribution varies with context (RH), principles can still be formulated to capture this variability by including holism clauses. RH defeats atomistic principles but not holistic ones; hence PE does not follow from RH alone.

questions.q: How does holistic generalism's mode of moral explanation differ from the particularist's according to Lecture 11?
options: HG explains by listing consequences; particularism explains by listing rules
HG explains by subsumption (showing an action falls under a holistic principle); particularism explains by narration (showing how the particular features of this concrete situation add up here)
HG explains by reference to virtues; particularism explains by reference to consequences
HG explains by deductive inference; particularism explains by intuition
HG and particularism offer identical explanations
questions.explain: HG: moral explanation works by subsuming the case under a principle (even a holistic one). Particularism: to explain why an action is wrong is to narrate how the morally relevant features of this particular situation combine in this context. The particularist holds that narrative explanation does not require principles.

questions.q: What is the practical consequence of the particularist's Principle Abstinence thesis?
options: Agents must always derive actions from rules

Agents should rely on direct attention to the particulars of each case rather than applying principles mechanically, because principles can distort attention and cause premature closure on the wrong verdict
Agents should consult only experts for moral verdicts
Agents should develop their own private principles
Agents should always defer to moral intuition over argument
questions.explain: PA holds that exclusive or over-heavy reliance on principles is epistemically dangerous: principles can focus attention on the wrong features and lead to premature, incorrect verdicts. The right method is sustained, open attention to the particular features of the situation.

questions.q: What is 'moral discernment' in particularism?
options: The ability to choose between competing moral theories
A trained capacity to notice morally relevant features and understand their relations, developed through experience, comparison, reflection, and moral education
An innate moral faculty that operates independently of experience
The application of a single master principle to cases
The ability to resolve moral dilemmas by intuition alone
questions.explain: Moral discernment is the cultivated perceptual and cognitive capacity to see which features of a situation are morally relevant and how they relate. It is developed through experience and reflection, not through learning rules. Particularism endorses this as the primary moral skill.

questions.q: What is the 'Practical Grammar' metaphor in moral particularism?
options: Morality is best understood as a formal logical system

Just as learning a language involves learning how words are used across contexts (not just definitions), moral concepts are learned by understanding their use across cases — with sensitivity to context, tone, and boundaries
Moral learning requires studying philosophical grammar
Moral rules have the structure of grammatical rules
Morality is inherently linguistic and cannot be separated from language
questions.explain: The practical grammar metaphor suggests that moral concepts (like 'cruelty') cannot be captured by a single definition applied mechanically. We learn to recognise obvious cruelty, subtle cruelty, justified harshness, and apparent kindness that is cruel — through exposure to varied cases, not through a rule.

questions.q: What is 'Case Comparison' and how does it function in particularist moral reasoning?
options: Comparing legal cases to derive moral precedents

Placing the case under consideration alongside other cases (malicious leak, whistleblowing, medical emergency, etc.) to illuminate morally relevant similarities and differences — without mechanically determining a verdict
Comparing different moral theories to select the correct one
Comparing consequences of different actions in a case
Comparing the views of virtuous persons about a case
questions.explain: Case comparison involves examining the case in question alongside structurally related cases with different verdicts (e.g., disclosure of confidential information in different contexts). This helps identify which features are doing the moral work — without delivering a mechanical verdict.

questions.q: What does the Universal Relevance Thesis (URT) claim?
options: Every moral principle applies universally across cultures
If a feature is morally relevant in one case, it is morally relevant wherever it appears
Universal principles always override particular judgments
Moral reasons have the same weight in all contexts
All persons are morally relevant in the same way
questions.explain: URT holds that moral relevance is not context-dependent: a feature that matters morally in one situation will matter morally wherever it occurs. This contrasts with particularism's view that features can be morally irrelevant in some contexts.

questions.q: What does the Polarity Thesis (PT) claim?
options: Moral features have two poles (positive and negative) that are fixed
If a feature counts in favour of an action in one case, it always counts in favour wherever it appears — its valence is stable
Positive and negative moral reasons always balance each other out
Moral features are always either all positive or all negative
Moral polarity is determined by cultural norms
questions.explain: PT holds that the directional contribution of a feature is fixed: a feature that supports an action in one context always supports actions in any context. This is the thesis directly challenged by particularism's concept of reversal.

questions.q: What role does Lecture 11 say principles can legitimately play, even conceding much to particularism?
options: Principles can serve as absolute constraints on action
Principles can serve as useful heuristics and guides — especially in professional ethics (medical ethics, public service) where they offer practical guidance — but should not replace discernment
Principles can replace discernment in all institutional settings
Principles should be abandoned altogether in applied ethics
Principles are only useful in purely philosophical discourse
questions.explain: The lecture acknowledges that principles are practically useful as guides, especially in professional contexts where explicit rules (informed consent, confidentiality, fairness) provide necessary structure. The particularist caveat is that principles should guide without replacing the capacity for discernment.

questions.q: How does particularism allow for moral learning if it rejects the primacy of principles?
options: By memorising a large set of specific cases and their verdicts mechanically
By learning patterns of moral relevance from cases — developing sensitivity to which features matter and how through exposure, comparison, and reflection
By deriving principles from cases and then applying them
By deferring to experts who have mastered the principles
By treating each case as entirely novel with no reference to prior experience
questions.explain: Particularism endorses learning from cases: not as a mechanical database of precedents but as the development of a cultivated sensitivity to morally relevant patterns. This is the 'practical grammar' model — learning how moral concepts apply across varied contexts.

questions.q: What is the 'case of lying' example meant to show in Lecture 11?
options: That lying is always wrong regardless of context

That lying can be morally relevant in favour (protecting from violence), against (job application fraud), irrelevant (harmless game), or context-dependent (doctor withholding harsh truth) — illustrating that a single feature has variable contribution
That lying always involves reversal rather than silencing
That all lies are equally bad morally
That the generalist can handle all cases of lying with a single principle
questions.explain: The four cases of lying illustrate reasons holism: lying can count in favour (protecting from unjust violence), count against (deception on a job application), be morally irrelevant (harmless game), or have a contribution that depends on further context (withholding harsh truth). The same feature — lying — has variable moral contribution.

questions.q: What does the Generic Generalist Thesis (GGT) claim?
options: There are infinitely many morally relevant features
There is a small set of fundamental morally relevant features that explain the rightness and wrongness of actions, and moral principles express these features
Every culture has its own fundamental moral features
Moral features are not explicable by any finite set of principles
General verdicts always override particular intuitions
questions.explain: GGT holds that morality is grounded in a finite, manageable set of fundamental features (e.g., harm, promise-breaking, fairness). These features explain moral status, and principles express them. The particularist denies this: features' contributions vary too much for a fixed set of principles to capture.

questions.q: What is 'double defeasibility' in holistic generalism?
options: The view that two conflicting duties both cancel out
The structure of holistic principles: they are defeasible in two ways — by silencing/reversal (holism clause) and by outweighing (overriding clause) — making them doubly sensitive to context
The view that both atomism and holism have exceptions
The view that two rounds of reasoning are needed to reach a moral verdict
The view that principles and intuitions can both be defeated
questions.explain: Holistic principles are 'doubly defeasible': first, the contribution of the feature may itself be altered or eliminated by context (holism clause — reversal, silencing, enabling, disabling); second, even if the feature contributes normally, it may be outweighed by opposing considerations (overriding clause).

questions.q: The lecture states 'particularism does not reject moral learning'. What does this mean?
options: Particularists hold that moral knowledge is impossible

Particularists hold that agents learn morality by developing moral discernment through cases and experience, not by memorising principles — moral learning is possible and real but proceeds differently from the generalist model
Particularists hold that learning principles is the foundation of moral development
Particularists hold that only explicit instruction in principles counts as moral learning
Particularists hold that moral learning is identical to legal education
questions.explain: Particularism is not moral scepticism or nihilism. It holds that genuine moral learning occurs — through developing sensitivity to morally relevant features, understanding how they interact, and building judgment through exposure to cases. The particularist model is experiential rather than principle-based.

questions.q: Can Principle Abstinence (PA) exist without Principle Eliminativism (PE), and vice versa?
options: No — PA and PE are logically equivalent
Yes — one can hold PE (no true principles) without PA, or hold PA (don't rely on principles) without PE (some principles may be true but still misguide). They are logically independent
PA entails PE but PE does not entail PA
PE entails PA but PA does not entail PE
They are mutually exclusive and cannot both be held
questions.explain: The lecture explicitly states it is possible to have PE without PA or PA without PE. PE is a metaphysical thesis (no true principles); PA is a practical/methodological thesis (don't rely on principles for guidance). Holding both is common but neither entails the other.
